Work to a sound order: pre-wet the floor, apply the cleaning solution, allow it to dwell, agitate, rinse, then recover. Each step has a purpose, and skipping or rushing one undermines the others.

The method, step by step

Pre-wetting the floor stops a dry, porous grout line from drinking up your cleaning solution unevenly and gives a more consistent dwell. Apply the solution evenly across the area you can realistically work before it dries. Let it dwell for the time the product label and SDS/TDS specify; dwell is what lets the chemistry loosen soil so agitation does not have to fight it. Then agitate, using the right tool for the area, the Dirty Grout Demon brushes for detail and edges and the Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head for open floor, to break soil free from inside the grout. Rinse thoroughly to flush the suspended soil out of the grout, and recover the dirty solution so it does not dry back onto the surface.

Residue control

Residue is the most common reason a cleaned floor looks good wet and disappointing dry. Leftover chemical or suspended soil dries as a film that dulls the tile, holds onto new soil, and can affect how the surface feels underfoot. Control it by applying only as much product as you can rinse and recover, rinsing properly, and recovering well. If in doubt, an extra rinse pass is cheaper than a callback. Keep your dwell within the label's guidance so the product does not dry on the floor before you can agitate and rinse it.

Confirming slip resistance after cleaning (AS 4663)

Cleaning, and any change to a floor's surface condition, can affect how slip-resistant it is. For a floor that is already in service, slip resistance is measured under AS 4663, which covers existing, in-service surfaces, as distinct from AS 4586, which classifies new surfaces before installation. The practical point for tile and grout work is that AS 4663 testing is the correct basis to confirm a finished floor still meets the slip-resistance class the site is aiming for. If a contract or site sets a target slip class, AS 4663 measurement is how you verify the cleaned floor against it.

Be careful about what these standards do and do not do. AS 4586 and AS 4663 classify and measure slip resistance only. They do not set cure, dry or re-entry times, and you should not imply that they do. They also do not tell you when it is safe to walk on a floor. While a floor is wet during and after cleaning it is a slip and access hazard, so isolate the area and use signage until it is dry and ready for use. For recommended minimum slip classifications by location type, the standards and supporting guidance such as HB 198 and the NCC are the source to consult directly; this lesson does not quote specific rating numbers.

Tie it together with documentation. A school or childcare contract values evidence as much as outcome, so record your method, the products used with their current SDS, and any slip-resistance verification you carry out. That record is what demonstrates you cleaned the floor safely and to the standard the client expects.

resources.label: Standards Australia — AS 4663 (slip resistance of existing pedestrian surfaces)

lessons.title: Knowledge check: tile & grout
lessons.contentBody:
Use this short self-check to confirm what you have taken from this module. Each question can be answered from the two lessons above. Choose one answer (A–D) per question, then check the answers and rationales at the end.

  • Q1. Why do grout lines tend to look dingy even when the tiles look clean?
    A. Grout is always a darker colour than tile
    B. Grout is porous and traps soil below the surface, while the denser tile face rinses clean
    C. Tiles are never sealed and grout always is
    D. Mopping cleans grout more effectively than tile
  • Q2. Which CCW tool is best suited to production cleaning across an open tiled floor with contained clean-and-recover in one pass?
    A. Dirty Grout Demon hand brush
    B. A flat mop
    C. Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head
    D. A dry dust mop
  • Q3. What does the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) for an alkaline hard-floor product such as Defender SV (AP672) provide?
    A. The required slip-resistance class for the site
    B. Hazards, PPE, safe handling and storage, and first-aid measures
    C. A guaranteed re-entry time for the floor
    D. The contract cleaning schedule
  • Q4. Which statement about AS 4663 is correct?
    A. It classifies new surfaces before installation
    B. It sets the re-entry time after cleaning
    C. It measures slip resistance of existing, in-service surfaces and can confirm a cleaned floor against a target slip class
    D. It mandates a specific cleaning chemical
  • Q5. What is the correct order for a sound tile and grout cleaning method?
    A. Recover, rinse, agitate, dwell, apply, pre-wet
    B. Pre-wet, apply, dwell, agitate, rinse, recover
    C. Apply, recover, pre-wet, rinse, dwell, agitate
    D. Agitate, apply, dwell, pre-wet, recover, rinse

Answers

  • Q1 — B. Grout is porous and absorbs and traps soil below the surface, whereas the denser tile face rinses clean, so grout looks dingy first.
  • Q2 — C. The Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head combines pressurised solution with a contained spinning action to clean and recover in one controlled pass over open floor.
  • Q3 — B. An SDS sets out hazards, PPE, safe handling and storage, and first-aid measures; it does not set the site's slip class or re-entry time.
  • Q4 — C. AS 4663 measures slip resistance of existing surfaces and is the correct basis to confirm a cleaned floor against a target slip class; it does not classify new surfaces or set re-entry times.
  • Q5 — B. The sound sequence is pre-wet, apply, dwell per the label, agitate, rinse, then recover.

modules.title: Vinyl Strip & Seal — the high-risk job

lessons.title: The strip-and-seal process, end to end
lessons.contentBody:
Vinyl and resilient floor strip-and-seal is one of the highest-risk jobs in commercial floor care. It uses caustic chemistry, leaves a wet and slippery surface, and depends on getting a sequence of steps right in the correct order. This lesson walks through the generic process end to end so you understand what each stage is for. The exact details for any step — dilution, coat counts, drying between coats — always come from the product's Safety Data Sheet (SDS) and Technical Data Sheet (TDS), never from memory or habit.

At a high level, the generic process runs: strip the old finish, then rinse and neutralise, then dry the floor, then apply multiple thin coats of sealer and finish with drying between each coat. Each stage prepares the surface for the next, so skipping or rushing a step undermines everything that follows.

Mapping the stages to a real Actichem system

We can map the generic process to a real Australian product line from the CCW / Actichem catalogue. Every product named here has a current SDS you must obtain and read before use.

  • Strip the old finish: Actichem Instastrip (SDS AP400) or Actichem Power Strip (SDS AP404). These strippers remove the existing sealer and finish layers. They are typically alkaline/caustic, which is why the safety lesson that follows matters so much.
  • Rinse and neutralise / prep: Actichem Pre-Seal / Preseal Pro (SDS AP171). After stripping, the floor is rinsed and the surface is prepared and neutralised so the new coats bond properly and no stripper residue is left behind.
  • Apply sealer: Actichem Instaseal (SDS AP420). The sealer goes down first as the base layer or layers.
  • Apply finish: Actichem Instagloss (SDS AP422). The finish / gloss coats build the protective, presentable surface on top of the sealer.

The drying stage between stripping and sealing, and the drying between each sealer and finish coat, is critical. A floor that is not dry, or coats applied before the previous coat is ready, will not perform. How long that takes is product-specific.

Why every step defers to the SDS and TDS

The specifics of every step — how much to dilute the stripper, how many coats to apply, how thin each coat should be, and how long to wait between coats — are set by the manufacturer for that exact product. They live in the SDS and TDS, not in this lesson. Two strippers, or two sealers, can behave very differently, so treating one product's process as a universal rule is a real hazard.

In a school or childcare facility this discipline matters even more. The area must be planned and isolated for the full duration of the job, and the work is usually scheduled into deep-clean or holiday programs rather than squeezed into an occupied day. Read the SDS and TDS first, build the job plan around what they say, and keep a current SDS for every product on site in your hazardous chemicals register. If a product lacks a current SDS, obtain it from the supplier before you start.

resources.label: SafeWork Australia — Using safety data sheets

lessons.title: Caustic stripper safety: PPE, ventilation, isolation and signage
lessons.contentBody:
Strippers used in vinyl strip-and-seal work are typically alkaline/caustic. That single fact drives the whole safety approach for this stage. Caustic chemistry can burn skin and eyes, give off vapours, and react with the floor — and the wet stripped floor itself becomes a serious slip and access hazard. This lesson covers how to control those risks. Always anchor your controls to your work health and safety (WHS) duties and to the specific stripper's Safety Data Sheet (SDS).

Your WHS starting point

Under the model WHS framework, a PCBU that uses, handles or stores hazardous chemicals must obtain and keep a current SDS for each one and keep it accessible to workers at the workplace. A hazardous chemicals register, with a current SDS for each chemical, must be prepared, maintained and accessible. Decanted or transferred containers must still be correctly labelled. Note that Victoria operates under its own OHS Act and Regulations rather than the model WHS framework, so check with your state regulator — SafeWork NSW, WorkSafe Victoria or WHSQ — for your jurisdiction.

The SDS for products such as Actichem Instastrip (SDS AP400) or Actichem Power Strip (SDS AP404) is your primary source for the specific PPE, handling, storage and first-aid measures for that stripper. Read it before the job, not during an incident.

Controls for caustic strippers

  • PPE: Protect skin and eyes. Caustic strippers warrant appropriate gloves, eye protection and clothing as specified in the product SDS. Treat splashes seriously.
  • Ventilation: Provide ventilation as the SDS directs. Stripping is wet, chemical work indoors, so airflow matters.
  • Spill control: Have spill control ready. Confine spills, clean them up promptly, and follow the SDS for the correct method and disposal.
  • Eye and skin contact: Know the first-aid response in the SDS before you start so you can act immediately if contact happens.

The wet floor is a hazard in its own right

A floor that has been stripped or freshly recoated is wet and slippery, and it stays a slip and access hazard while it is wet or curing. Anyone walking onto it risks a fall, and foot traffic also ruins the finish. The control is simple and non-negotiable: isolate the area and sign it clearly. Keep people out for the whole duration the floor is wet or curing.

This is especially important in schools and childcare centres, where children may not read or heed a sign. Physical isolation — barriers, locked-off rooms, scheduling into holiday or out-of-hours periods — protects children far better than a sign alone. Plan the access control before you open the first drum of stripper. Do not state a fixed time for how long to keep people off the floor; how long the floor stays wet or curing is product-specific and comes from the SDS and TDS, not from a rule of thumb.

resources.label: SafeWork Australia — Using safety data sheets

lessons.title: Sealing and finishing — cure & re-entry come from the SDS/TDS, not guesswork
lessons.contentBody:
Once the floor is stripped, rinsed, neutralised and fully dry, you build the new surface back up by applying coats of sealer and then finish. The craft here is in applying thin, even coats and allowing each coat to dry before the next goes down. The single most important safety and quality rule of this lesson is this: do not rely on any fixed dry, cure, re-entry or buffing time. Those times are product-specific, and the only correct source for them is the product's Technical Data Sheet (TDS) and Safety Data Sheet (SDS).

Thin, even coats with drying between them

Sealer and finish are built up in multiple thin coats rather than one thick layer. Thin coats dry and cure more predictably and produce a more durable, even surface. Using a real Actichem system, the sealer base is Actichem Instaseal (SDS AP420) and the finish or gloss coats are Actichem Instagloss (SDS AP422). The earlier prep step — Actichem Pre-Seal / Preseal Pro (SDS AP171) — sets the surface up so these coats bond well.

Between each coat the floor must be allowed to dry. Applying a coat before the one beneath it is ready will trap solvent or water, cloud the finish, and weaken adhesion. How thin each coat should be, how many coats to apply, and how long to wait between them are all product-specific values found in the TDS.

Why you must never state a fixed cure or re-entry time

It is tempting to carry a number in your head — "finishes are walkable after so long." Resist it. Different sealers and finishes cure at different rates, and that rate is affected by temperature, humidity, airflow and coat thickness. The manufacturer publishes the correct dry-between-coats, full-cure and re-entry guidance for that exact product in its TDS. Your job is to read it and plan the work around it.

Practically, that means planning a strip-and-seal job backwards from the TDS: count the coats, add the manufacturer's drying time between each, allow for full cure before the floor returns to service, and only then commit to a return date with the client. In a school or childcare facility this is why such work is scheduled into deep-clean or holiday programs — there has to be enough closed time for the floor to dry and cure properly with the area isolated and signed.

What to do on site

  • Obtain and read the current SDS and TDS for every sealer and finish before you start.
  • Apply thin, even coats and let each dry per the TDS before the next.
  • Keep the area isolated and signed for the whole drying and curing period.
  • Build the schedule and the client's re-entry date from the TDS, not from memory.
  • If a product lacks a current SDS or TDS, obtain it from the supplier rather than guessing.

resources.label: SafeWork Australia — Using safety data sheets

lessons.title: Verifying slip resistance to a target class (AS 4663)
lessons.contentBody:
A freshly sealed and finished floor can have a different slip resistance from the bare or previously finished surface. A glossy new finish may feel and behave differently underfoot, especially when wet. So a strip-and-seal job is not truly finished until you have confirmed the floor still meets the slip-resistance target that the site requires. This lesson explains where AS 4663 fits — and, just as importantly, what it does not do.

Two standards, two jobs

There are two related Australian Standards for slip resistance, and they apply to different stages:

  • AS 4586:2013 classifies the slip resistance of new pedestrian surfaces. This is laboratory or pre-installation testing — it is about products and materials before they go down.
  • AS 4663:2013 measures the slip resistance of existing surfaces in service, on site.

Because a strip-and-seal job produces an existing, in-service floor, AS 4663 is the correct basis to re-verify slip resistance after stripping and sealing. It lets you measure the finished floor as it actually is and check it against the target slip class for that location.

What AS 4663 does and does not do

AS 4663 measures and classifies the slip resistance of the surface. That is its job. It is essential to be clear about its limits:

  • AS 4663 does not set cure or re-entry times. It tells you how slip-resistant the floor is, not when it is safe to reopen. Re-entry timing comes from the product TDS and SDS, as covered earlier in this module.
  • AS 4586 and AS 4663 classify and measure only — they do not impose a particular outcome by themselves.

For guidance on what slip-resistance classification is recommended for a given location type, the recommended minimum classifications are set out in companion guidance such as SA HB 198:2014, which the National Construction Code references for new building work. Quote that guidance and the NCC directly rather than carrying a specific rating value in your head, because the appropriate target depends on the location and the current documents.

Putting it into practice

Decide the target slip class with reference to HB 198 and the NCC and any requirement in the site's contract. After the finish has cured per the TDS, arrange measurement of the finished floor in accordance with AS 4663 to confirm it still meets that target. In a school or childcare setting, where wet entryways and spill-prone areas carry real fall risk for children, this verification step turns "the floor looks great" into documented evidence that it performs to the agreed class. Keep that result with your job records as part of the documentation a contract expects.

resources.label: Standards Australia — AS 4663:2013 (slip resistance of existing surfaces)

lessons.title: Knowledge check: strip & seal
lessons.contentBody:
This short self-check helps you confirm the key safety and process points from this module. Choose one answer (A–D) for each question, then check the answers below.

1. In the generic strip-and-seal process, what is the correct order of stages?

  • A. Apply finish, then strip, then rinse
  • B. Strip old finish, rinse/neutralise, dry, then apply multiple thin sealer/finish coats with drying between coats
  • C. Dry, strip, apply one thick coat, reopen
  • D. Neutralise, apply finish, strip, rinse

2. Where do the correct dry, cure and re-entry times for a sealer or finish come from?

  • A. A fixed industry rule you can memorise
  • B. AS 4663
  • C. The product's SDS and TDS
  • D. Whatever the previous job used

3. Vinyl floor strippers such as Actichem Instastrip (SDS AP400) are typically:

  • A. Acidic and harmless
  • B. Alkaline/caustic, requiring PPE and ventilation
  • C. Food-grade and skin-safe
  • D. Inert and non-hazardous

4. Which standard is the correct basis to verify slip resistance of the finished, in-service floor?

  • A. AS 4586:2013 (new surfaces, lab/pre-installation)
  • B. AS 4663:2013 (existing surfaces, on site)
  • C. Neither — slip resistance cannot be measured
  • D. The SDS sets the slip class

5. While a stripped or freshly recoated floor is wet or curing, the correct control is to:

  • A. Let normal foot traffic continue
  • B. Rely on a single warning sign in an occupied childcare room
  • C. Isolate the area and sign it, keeping people out for the whole wet/curing period
  • D. Open windows and reopen immediately

Answers

  • 1 — B. The generic process is strip, rinse/neutralise, dry, then multiple thin sealer/finish coats with drying between coats.
  • 2 — C. Dry, cure and re-entry times are product-specific and come only from the SDS/TDS, never from a fixed rule.
  • 3 — B. Strippers are typically alkaline/caustic, so PPE and ventilation per the SDS are required.
  • 4 — B. AS 4663:2013 measures existing in-service surfaces, so it is the basis for verifying the finished floor.
  • 5 — C. The wet or curing floor is a slip and access hazard, so isolate and sign it; in childcare, physical isolation beats a sign alone.

lessons.title: Pressure-washing technique, surfaces and pressures
lessons.contentBody:
Pressure washing looks straightforward, but on a school or childcare site it is a job that rewards judgement. The same machine that strips chewing gum off a path can also gouge soft pavers, etch concrete, blow render off a wall, or drive a stream of grit and water into someone's leg. The skill is matching the pressure and the nozzle to the surface in front of you, and setting up the area so nobody gets hurt.

Start by reading the surface. Hard, sound concrete tolerates higher pressure than older or decorative surfaces. Pavers, especially sand-jointed or clay pavers, can be displaced or pitted, and aggressive blasting can wash out jointing sand and leave a loose, uneven path. Soft-fall and rubberised play surfaces, painted line-marking, timber, and anything coated or sealed are easily damaged and usually call for the lowest practical pressure or a different method altogether. If you are unsure how a surface will respond, test a small, out-of-the-way patch first and check the result before committing to the whole area.

Nozzle choice controls how the water hits the surface. A narrow, tight jet concentrates force and is the most likely to cut or scar; a wider fan spreads the same pressure over more area and is gentler. As a rule, work with the widest fan that still does the job, keep the nozzle moving, and hold a consistent distance and angle rather than driving the tip hard into one spot. Backing the nozzle off the surface and increasing the fan angle are both ways to reduce intensity when you see a surface starting to mark.

Treat the equipment as a genuine injury hazard. High-pressure water can break skin and inject contaminants, so never point the lance at yourself or anyone else, never try to clear a blockage with the trigger live, and wear the personal protective equipment the task calls for — eye protection, sturdy footwear and hearing protection where the machine is loud. Hoses, leads and a wet, slippery work zone are all trip and slip risks; keep them tidy and out of walkways.

Working around children's outdoor areas adds a layer that ordinary commercial sites do not have. The expectation under the childcare quality framework is that children are protected from harm and injury, so the work needs to be planned so children are nowhere near it. Schedule pressure washing for times the area is vacated — before children arrive, after they leave, or during holiday and deep-clean programs when outdoor spaces are not in use. Physically cordon off the work zone so there is no casual access, keep equipment, leads and chemicals secured, and make sure the surface is dry, rinsed and safe to walk on before the area is handed back. Confirm timing and access with the site contact rather than assuming a window is clear.

Finally, remember that pressure washing on these sites is never just a cleaning task — it also generates wash water that has to be controlled. How you set up the area, where the water runs, and how you capture it are covered in the next lesson, and they should be planned before you pull the trigger, not after.

lessons.title: “The drain is just for rain”: capturing run-off and trade-waste to sewer
lessons.contentBody:
There are two drainage systems on most Australian sites, and confusing them is one of the easiest ways to cause real environmental harm. Stormwater drains — the grates in the carpark, the pits along the path, the kerbside gully — are built to carry rain. In most areas that water flows untreated, straight to creeks, rivers and the sea. As the regulators put it, the drain is just for rain. The sewer is the other system: it goes to a treatment plant. The whole job of run-off control is making sure your wash water ends up in the right one.

Pressure-washing run-off is not clean water. It carries detergents, grease, grit, paint flakes, gum and whatever else was on the surface, and that mix must not be allowed to enter stormwater drains. The practical answer is to confine the area, collect the wash water, and direct it to sewer. Containment can be done with booms or absorbent socks ringing the work zone, with temporary bunding to pond the water, and with mats or covers over nearby stormwater pits so nothing escapes down them. Recovery is usually by a wet vacuum or vacuum-recovery system that lifts the water for proper disposal rather than letting it run off across the ground.

Getting the water to sewer is not as simple as tipping it down the nearest gully trap. Discharging trade wastewater to the sewer needs prior written approval from the water authority — commonly called a trade-waste agreement. That approval sets out what may be discharged and on what conditions, and it must be in place before you discharge, not arranged afterwards. The water authority is the body that decides whether, where and how your wash water can enter the sewer, so contact them early when you are quoting a recurring contract.

Behind all of this sits a general environmental duty: the broad obligation to take reasonable and practicable steps to prevent or minimise harm to the environment from your activities. In Victoria, for example, the EPA treats contaminated wash water as industrial waste that must be captured and kept out of stormwater, and the general environmental duty applies. That duty does not wait for someone to inspect you — it is on you to plan the job so run-off is controlled from the start.

Letting contaminated water reach waterways is a pollution offence, and polluting-waters offences can carry significant penalties. To give a sense of scale only, you may see figures cited in the tens or hundreds of thousands of dollars for serious water-pollution offences — but treat any such number as illustrative and jurisdiction-specific. The actual offences, defences and penalty amounts differ between states and territories and change over time, so the only reliable figures are the current ones from the relevant Act.

The takeaway for a school or childcare contract is to design run-off control into the method before you start: know where the stormwater pits are and protect them, contain and recover the wash water, arrange the trade-waste approval needed to discharge to sewer, and confirm the specific requirements with the local water authority and EPA. When in doubt, ask the water authority and the EPA in your jurisdiction rather than guessing — the rules are local, and they are the ones who set them.

resources.label: Sydney Water – trade wastewater and the sewer
EPA NSW – stormwater and water pollution

lessons.title: Knowledge check: pressure washing
lessons.contentBody:
Use this short self-check to test the key points from this module. Choose one answer (A–D) for each question, then check yourself against the answers below.

1. You are pressure washing a sand-jointed paver path at a childcare centre. Which approach best avoids surface damage?

  • A. Use the narrowest jet at maximum pressure to finish faster
  • B. Use the widest practical fan, keep the nozzle moving, and test a small area first
  • C. Hold the tight jet on each joint to clear it fully
  • D. Increase pressure until the jointing sand washes out

2. When should outdoor pressure washing at a childcare centre be scheduled?

  • A. During outdoor play so staff can supervise the cleaner
  • B. Whenever the cleaner is available, with children kept a few metres back
  • C. When the area is vacated, with the work zone cordoned off
  • D. It does not matter as long as the equipment is new

3. “The drain is just for rain” means that:

  • A. Stormwater drains generally flow untreated to waterways, so contaminated wash water must not enter them
  • B. Stormwater is treated before release, so any run-off is fine
  • C. Rainwater is the only thing that may legally enter the sewer
  • D. Wash water can go down any drain after it rains

4. Before discharging pressure-washing wash water (trade wastewater) to the sewer, you must:

  • A. Dilute it with clean water until it looks clear
  • B. Have prior written approval – a trade-waste agreement – from the water authority
  • C. Notify the school after the job is done
  • D. Nothing – the sewer accepts any liquid waste

5. A colleague quotes you an exact dollar penalty for a water-pollution offence. How should you treat it?

  • A. As a fixed national figure that applies everywhere
  • B. As illustrative and jurisdiction-specific – verify against the current Act
  • C. As the maximum the EPA can ever impose
  • D. As irrelevant, since penalties are never enforced

Answers

  • 1. B — the widest practical fan, a moving nozzle and a test patch minimise the risk of pitting pavers or washing out jointing sand.
  • 2. C — work when the area is vacated and cordoned off so children are protected from harm.
  • 3. A — stormwater generally flows untreated to waterways, so contaminated wash water must be kept out of stormwater drains.
  • 4. B — discharging trade wastewater to sewer needs prior written approval (a trade-waste agreement) from the water authority.
  • 5. B — penalty figures are illustrative and jurisdiction-specific; always verify against the current Act in your state or territory.

lessons.title: How to run a floor-care job: the seven-step service workflow
lessons.contentBody:
The earlier modules taught you the four service areas — carpet, tile and grout, vinyl strip and seal, and pressure washing — surface by surface. This module reorganises the same work as a repeatable seven-step field workflow you run on every job, whatever the surface. Think of it as your run-sheet from the moment you arrive on site to the moment you hand the floor back.

  1. Inspect — walk the site, read the environment, capture the starting condition.
  2. Identify — confirm the floor type, finish and soil so you choose the right method.
  3. Report — document findings, scope and recommendations the client can act on.
  4. Clean — the routine or periodic clean for the surface you identified.
  5. Seal — apply sealer or finish to a prepared floor.
  6. Strip & seal — the full strip-and-reseal cycle when a floor is past cleaning alone.
  7. Post-care — return-to-service, wet-floor management and the ongoing maintenance plan.

Each step below is a checklist you can follow on site. Two disciplines run through all of them, exactly as in the rest of the course. First, the specifics come from the source, not from a fixed rule: dilution ratios, dwell, coat counts, and dry, cure or re-entry times come from each product's Safety Data Sheet (SDS) and Technical Data Sheet (TDS), and slip-resistance targets come from the relevant standard — never from a number quoted from memory. Second, you are working around children: low-tox product selection, current SDS on file, correct Working With Children checks, and hard isolation of any wet or treated floor are not optional extras — they are the job.

This is professional development and contract-readiness training. It is not an IICRC-accredited course and does not award IICRC Continuing Education Credits (CEC). Where an industry standard such as IICRC S100 is referenced, treat it as a recognised standard of care that supports your judgement — not as law, and not a substitute for the SDS/TDS, the Australian Standards, or a site's own rules.

resources.label: Safe Work Australia — Safety data sheets

lessons.title: Step 1 — Pre-job inspection
lessons.contentBody:
Service Procedure — Pre-Job Site & Floor Inspection

A school or childcare contract assumes you inspect before you quote. The pre-job inspection confirms the scope, checks access, power and water, reads the condition of every floor, and produces the dated record and readiness documentation the contract expects. Work through the steps in order and finish with the inspection record that feeds your quote and method statement.

  1. Confirm scope and the current contract specification. Separate routine scope (light, frequent cleaning) from periodic scope (hard-floor strip-and-seal, carpet hot water extraction) and list every surface in play — carpet, tile and grout, vinyl and resilient floors, and external hard surfaces. Published standards such as School Infrastructure NSW are directional only; the binding detail is the current contract specification, so verify against it.
  2. Confirm site credentials and child-safe requirements before you attend. Child-related work generally requires a Working With Children check, administered per state and territory (NSW WWCC, VIC Working with Children Check, QLD Blue Card, ACT WWVP) and generally not transferable. Confirm each worker holds the correct current check and complete any site child-safe induction.
  3. Book the inspection around occupancy. Note the low-occupancy windows and term-break or holiday periods periodic work will need, and confirm access with the site contact rather than assuming a window is clear.
  4. Walk the site and map rooms, zones and access. Note occupancy patterns and entry/exit routes, identify where children spend time, and note where you can sequence the work room-by-room so each space can be treated, dried and reopened while you move on.
  5. Check services and utilities — water and power. Confirm water access and waste-water disposal points. Confirm power is 230 V / 50 Hz through 10 A GPOs protected by an RCD/safety switch, and that every lead and machine is within its AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval. For pressure washing, locate the stormwater pits and intended discharge point now, not on the day.
  6. Inspect each floor's condition (carpet pre-inspection per S100). For carpet, ANSI/IICRC S100 treats pre-inspection as part of the job: identify fibre and construction, read the soil load, and find pre-existing damage, staining, delamination or wear. For tile, note grout condition; for vinyl, note the existing finish; for external surfaces, note the material and how fragile it is. Photograph and record what you find.
  7. Assess WHS hazards and plan controls. Identify slip, trip, electrical, chemical and manual-handling hazards, note where wet floors will need isolation and signage and where ventilation is needed, and prepare a SWMS/JSEA for high-risk work such as caustic strip-and-seal or pressure washing.
  8. Confirm chemical and SDS readiness. List every chemical you will bring and confirm a current SDS (reviewed within the last 5 years) for each, in an accessible register (model WHS Regulation 346). Plan to correctly label any decanted container. Where an item lacks a current SDS, obtain it from the supplier — do not guess a code. Victoria uses its own OHS framework.
  9. Note slip-resistance targets and how you will verify them. Where a surface will change (tile and grout, and vinyl after sealing), note any target slip class. An existing, in-service floor is measured under AS 4663 (AS 4586 classifies new surfaces before installation). These standards classify and measure only — they do not set re-entry times. Consult HB 198 and the NCC for recommended classifications rather than quoting a rating.
  10. Plan run-off control for any pressure washing. Locate the stormwater pits (they generally flow untreated to waterways — the drain is just for rain), plan containment and recovery, and confirm with the water authority whether a trade-waste agreement is needed before discharging wash water to sewer. In Victoria, EPA Victoria treats contaminated wash water as industrial waste.
  11. Record pre-existing damage and set realistic client expectations. Document existing damage, permanent staining and wear before you start so a pre-existing problem is not later blamed on your visit. Flag realistic outcomes, and do not promise fixed dry, cure or re-entry times — they depend on the product and the conditions.
  12. Produce the inspection record and method/scheduling plan. Pull it into a written record that feeds the quote: surfaces and condition, hazards and controls, chemical and SDS readiness, slip-resistance and run-off plans, and a schedule that places periodic work in deep-clean or holiday windows and sequences occupied-site work with drying and isolation built in. Confirm against the current contract specification and the site's rules before committing dates.

Safety

  • Sign in, wear appropriate PPE, and stay clear of live classroom or play activity during the walk.
  • Identify slip, trip, electrical, chemical and manual-handling hazards, and note where wet or treated floors will need isolation and signage.
  • Confirm power is 230 V / 50 Hz through 10 A GPOs on an RCD/safety switch, and that leads and machines are within their AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval.
  • Confirm a current SDS (within 5 years) for every chemical, in an accessible register (model WHS Regulation 346); check WorkSafe Victoria for VIC contracts. Keep Poisons Information 13 11 26 with your SDS.
  • Prepare a SWMS/JSEA for high-risk work such as caustic strip-and-seal or pressure washing.

Child-safe

  • Confirm each worker holds the correct state or territory Working With Children check, plus any site child-safe induction — the WWC check is a starting point, not the whole picture.
  • Book the inspection and the work around occupancy; periodic work belongs in deep-clean or term-break/holiday programs.
  • Plan how the work zone will be physically separated, signed and kept off-limits while floors are wet or treated; keep children and pets away from equipment, leads and chemicals.
  • Note ventilation options so strippers, sealers and presprays are used with fresh air moving away from children.
  • Favour lower-tox, child-safe products for routine occupied work; reserve stronger chemistry for isolated deep-clean windows.

Standards

  • Model WHS Act and Regulations (Safe Work Australia, 2011) as adopted by your state or territory; Victoria uses its own OHS Act and Regulations.
  • Model WHS Regulation 346 (hazardous chemicals register); Schedule 11 manifest thresholds (generally not triggered by ordinary floor-care quantities).
  • AS/NZS 3760 (test-and-tag) for electrical leads and equipment.
  • ANSI/IICRC S100 (S100-2021) as a recognised standard of care for carpet pre-inspection — guidance, not law. No IICRC CECs are awarded and this is not an IICRC-accredited course.
  • AS 4586 (new pedestrian surfaces) and AS 4663 (existing/in-service surfaces); SA HB 198 and the NCC for recommended classifications.
  • NQS Quality Area 2 (childcare); state and territory Working With Children check legislation; state EPA and water-authority trade-waste requirements.

Document this

  • A written pre-job inspection record: surfaces and areas in scope, occupancy and scheduling notes, and access arrangements.
  • Dated photographs and notes of pre-existing damage, staining and wear.
  • The chemicals list with a current SDS for each in the register (Reg 346), including the decant-labelling plan.
  • Identified hazards and the SWMS/JSEA for high-risk tasks.
  • Any slip-resistance target and the plan to verify it under AS 4663; the run-off and trade-waste plan for pressure washing.
  • The method and scheduling plan (routine versus periodic scope) feeding the quote, verified against the current contract specification.

lessons.title: Step 2 — Surface & soil identification
lessons.contentBody:
Service Procedure — Surface & Soil Identification (Method Selection)

Choosing the right method starts with correctly identifying the surface in front of you and reading its soil and condition. This procedure takes you across the four service families a school or childcare contract expects — carpet, tile and grout, vinyl strip-and-seal, and external pressure washing — so you match method, tool and child-safe product to what is actually there rather than forcing one approach onto everything.

  1. Confirm the surface category first. School and childcare floor care falls into four families: carpet (a textile covering), tile and grout (a dense tile face over porous grout), vinyl and resilient floors (which may carry a sealer and finish), and external hard surfaces (concrete, pavers, soft-fall). Identify the surface before you reach for a tool or a chemical.
  2. Carpet — identify fibre and construction. Different fibres respond differently to heat, moisture and chemistry, and construction (cut pile, loop, density, backing) affects how soil is held and how quickly the carpet releases moisture. Identify what is in front of you so you can match method and product to the carpet. ANSI/IICRC S100 frames this as part of pre-inspection — a recognised standard of care, not law.
  3. Carpet — read the soil type and load. Soil ranges from dry particulate (grit and dust — the bulk of what lands in a school carpet) through oily and greasy residues, water-soluble spills, and spots needing targeted treatment. A large share of dry particulate comes out with thorough vacuuming first. The soil type tells you whether the answer is mechanical removal, chemistry, or both — and whether the load justifies deep extraction or a lighter maintenance clean.
  4. Tile and grout — identify tile type and grout porosity. The tile face is usually dense and sealed, so soil sits on top and rinses away; grout is porous and traps soil below the surface, which is why grout lines darken first. Confirm the tile type and condition tolerate your intended tool and chemistry. Greasy, oily soils on hard floors are typically tackled with alkaline chemistry.
  5. Vinyl and resilient — identify the existing finish and substrate. Identify whether there is an existing sealer and finish to strip and confirm the floor is genuinely resilient. Strip-and-seal is one of the highest-risk jobs — caustic chemistry, a wet and slippery surface — so identifying the finish and substrate drives both method and safety plan. The generic process is strip, rinse and neutralise, dry, then multiple thin sealer and finish coats with drying between coats; specifics come from each product's SDS and TDS.
  6. External hard surfaces — read the surface for pressure washing. Sound concrete tolerates higher pressure; sand-jointed or clay pavers can be displaced or pitted; soft-fall, rubberised play surfaces, painted line-marking, timber and coated surfaces are easily damaged and call for the lowest practical pressure or a different method. Match the nozzle to the surface — the widest fan that still does the job, kept moving at a consistent distance and angle.
  7. Test in an inconspicuous area. Test your intended method, chemistry or pressure on a small, out-of-the-way patch first and check the result before committing. Work spots before a general clean. A test patch is the cheapest insurance against damaging a floor you cannot easily put right.
  8. Match method to surface, soil and the occupied setting. For carpet, hot water extraction gives deeper soil removal but adds moisture and a longer return to service (heavy soil, with a drying window), while encapsulation is low-moisture with a faster return (occupied classrooms, a lighter clean). For tile and grout, pair chemistry with agitation and proper rinse and recovery. For vinyl, follow the strip / rinse-and-neutralise / dry / thin-coats sequence. For external surfaces, use the widest practical fan and keep the nozzle moving.
  9. Select a child-safe product matched to the job. Choose a lower-tox product for routine occupied work; reserve stronger chemistry (a caustic stripper) for periodic deep-clean or holiday work outside child contact. Read the current SDS and dilute strictly per the label. Real anchors from the Australian CCW/Actichem catalogue include presprays Clean Force (AP453) and Extracta Pro (AP457), spotters Bust (AP496) and GumGone (AP483), alkaline hard-floor cleaner Defender SV (AP672), and the strip-and-seal line Instastrip (AP400)/Power Strip (AP404), Pre-Seal (AP171), Instaseal (AP420) and Instagloss (AP422).
  10. Confirm Australian availability and a current SDS. Confirm the product and any 230 V equipment are available in Australia and that you hold a current SDS (within 5 years) for every chemical. Where an item lacks a current SDS, obtain it from the supplier before use — do not invent a code. Availability check: the catalogue products and 230 V machines named here (Actichem/CCW range; Polystar 13-inch orbital encapsulation scrubber, approximately 330 mm pad; Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head; Dirty Grout Demon brushes) were AU-available as at 8 July 2026 — re-confirm before you quote.
  11. Decide sequencing and a conditions-based drying approach. Sequence rooms so each is treated, dried and reopened while you move on, and never quote a fixed dry time — drying depends on the surface, the moisture applied, airflow, temperature and humidity. Place periodic, higher-moisture work in deep-clean or holiday windows where the space can be closed and isolated.
  12. Record the identification and method decision. Record the surface (fibre and construction for carpet; tile and grout condition; existing vinyl finish; external material), the soil type and load, the test-patch result, and the method, tool and product selected with its AP-code and current SDS. Note dilution comes from the label/SDS/TDS and drying is conditions-based, and record any slip-resistance target to be verified under AS 4663. This record feeds your method statement and the documentation the contract expects.

Safety

  • Read each product's current SDS for hazards, PPE, ventilation and first aid; recognise GHS pictograms (e.g. the corrosion diamond on a caustic stripper) as your cue to take PPE and ventilation seriously.
  • Match chemistry to the job: vinyl strippers are typically alkaline/caustic and warrant gloves, eye protection and ventilation; presprays, spotters and alkaline hard-floor cleaners each carry their own hazard profile.
  • Treat high-pressure water as an injury hazard — never point the lance at anyone, use the widest practical fan and a test patch, and wear eye, foot and hearing protection.
  • Confirm equipment runs on 230 V / 50 Hz via a 10 A GPO on an RCD/safety switch and is within its AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval.
  • Dilute strictly per the label/SDS/TDS — never stronger to be sure. Keep Poisons Information 13 11 26 with your SDS.

Child-safe

  • Choose lower-tox products for routine occupied work; reserve the harshest chemistry for periodic deep-clean or holiday windows away from child contact.
  • Re-entry timing is product-specific — follow the SDS/TDS and coordinate access with the centre; isolate and clearly sign wet or treated floors.
  • Match method to the occupancy window: encapsulation's faster return suits occupied classrooms, while hot water extraction and strip-and-seal need closed deep-clean or holiday windows.
  • Keep children and pets away from the work zone, equipment, leads and chemicals, and manage cords so they are never a trip hazard.

Standards

  • ANSI/IICRC S100 (S100-2021) as a recognised standard of care for identifying carpet fibre, construction and soil — guidance, not law. No IICRC CECs are awarded and this is not an IICRC-accredited course.
  • GHS hazard classification and Safe Work Australia SDS conventions; model WHS Regulation 346 (register) and correct labelling, including decanted containers.
  • AS 4586 (new pedestrian surfaces) and AS 4663 (existing/in-service surfaces) where a chosen method changes the surface; SA HB 198 and the NCC for recommended classifications.
  • AS/NZS 3760 (test-and-tag) for the electrical equipment selected.
  • NQS Quality Area 2 for child-safe product selection in childcare.

Document this

  • Each surface identified (fibre and construction for carpet; tile and grout condition; existing vinyl finish; external surface type) and the soil type and load read on it.
  • The test-patch result for any method, chemistry or pressure before full application.
  • The method, tool and product selected for each surface, with the product's AP-code and a current SDS in the register.
  • The dilution source noted as the label/SDS/TDS (not a fixed figure), and drying noted as conditions-based (not a fixed time).
  • Any slip-resistance target and the plan to verify it under AS 4663.
  • A dated Australian-availability note for the catalogue products and equipment selected.

lessons.title: Step 3 — Reporting
lessons.contentBody:
Service Procedure — Reporting

A client-ready report is what turns a finished job into evidence a school or childcare centre can file, audit and act on. This procedure builds a single report that documents your pre-inspection findings, the agreed scope, the condition of each surface, the products and controls used, and the completion status across all four service areas — carpet, tile and grout, vinyl strip and seal, and pressure washing. It is not a marketing document; it is the paperwork layer that separates a casual job from a contract a client keeps renewing.

  1. Open with the job identifiers. Record the contractor's legal name and ABN, the site name and address, the client and site contact, the contract or purchase-order reference, and the date(s) of attendance. State which service areas the visit covered, because a strip-and-seal report carries very different risk and documentation from a routine carpet clean.
  2. Record the pre-inspection findings. Write up the fibre and construction, soil load and any pre-existing damage for carpet; the tile type, grout condition and any target slip class for tile and grout; and the existing finish and substrate condition for vinyl. Noting pre-existing damage up front protects both the client and you.
  3. Capture dated before-condition evidence. Photograph each area before work begins, with enough context that every image is identifiable later, and keep the images dated and tied to the room or zone.
  4. State the agreed scope of works per surface. Set out the method chosen and why — hot water extraction versus encapsulation for carpet; pre-wet, apply, dwell, agitate, rinse and recover for tile and grout; strip, rinse and neutralise, dry, then multiple thin sealer and finish coats for vinyl. Make clear that dilution ratios, coat counts and any drying or re-entry steps come from each product's SDS and TDS, not from a figure in the report.
  5. List the products used and reference the SDS register. Record every chemical by product name and supplier code, and confirm a current SDS (no more than five years old) is held for each in an accessible register. Where a product lacked a current SDS, record that you obtained it from the supplier before use.
  6. Document the WHS controls you applied. Record the SWMS or JSEA, the PPE worn per each SDS, the ventilation, spill controls, and how wet or treated floors were isolated and signed — and that all portable equipment was within its AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval on RCD-protected supply.
  7. Record the child-safety measures. Record the workers' Working With Children checks and any site induction, the lower-tox product selection, the physical isolation and signage of wet or treated areas, and how the work was scheduled around occupancy or into a term break, framed as supporting NQS Quality Area 2.
  8. Record run-off control for any pressure washing. Document the containment, the recovery method, and the trade-waste approval from the water authority that allowed discharge to sewer, keeping wash water out of stormwater.
  9. Record slip-resistance verification where a floor was sealed. Note the target slip class (with reference to SA HB 198 and the NCC) and the AS 4663 result or arranged testing, and state clearly that AS 4663 measures slip resistance only and does not set re-entry times.
  10. Capture after-condition evidence and completion status. Photograph the finished areas from comparable positions and record each surface as complete, part-complete or deferred, flagging any floor still curing at the time of writing.
  11. State recommendations and follow-up. Turn observations into actionable recommendations — periodic deep treatments, matting improvements, a re-test interval — and point re-entry timing to the product SDS/TDS rather than quoting a fixed time.
  12. Add the limitations note, then review, sign and issue. Close with a short limitations statement, proofread for Australian spelling and consistency, sign and date, issue to the client, and retain a copy.

Safety

  • Anchor every control in the product SDS and your WHS duties; read the SDS before the job, not during an incident.
  • Record the SWMS or JSEA, PPE, ventilation and spill controls actually used — the report is evidence, not a formality.
  • Confirm and note that portable electrical equipment was within its AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval and run on RCD-protected 230 V / 10 A supply.
  • Never state a fixed dry, cure or re-entry time; those belong to the SDS/TDS. Poisons Information is 13 11 26.

Child-safe

  • Record each worker's state or territory Working With Children check plus any site-specific child-safe induction.
  • Document lower-tox product selection and label-rate dilution for occupied, child-occupied spaces.
  • Record how wet, freshly stripped or freshly sealed floors were physically isolated and signed and kept away from children — a sign alone is not enough.
  • Show the work was scheduled around occupancy or into a term break / holiday window, supporting NQS Quality Area 2.

Standards

  • Model WHS Regulations — Reg 346 register, current SDS (within 5 years) kept accessible, correct labelling including decanted containers; Victoria under its own OHS Act.
  • AS 4663 (existing/in-service surfaces) for verifying a sealed floor; AS 4586 (new surfaces) noted for contrast; SA HB 198 and the NCC for recommended slip classes.
  • AS/NZS 3760 for the test-and-tag status of portable equipment; GHS / Safe Work Australia for SDS structure.
  • ANSI/IICRC S100 as a recognised standard of care only — this service is NOT IICRC-accredited and awards no CECs; NQF / NQS Quality Area 2 (ACECQA) for childcare health and safety.

Document this

  • Report header (contractor, site, client, contract/PO reference, dates, service areas).
  • Pre-inspection findings, dated before-and-after photos, and method chosen per surface with specifics deferred to SDS/TDS.
  • Products-used list cross-referenced to the current SDS register.
  • WHS record (SWMS/JSEA, PPE, ventilation, isolation, AS/NZS 3760 equipment status) and child-safety record (WWC, induction, low-tox, scheduling).
  • Run-off / trade-waste record for pressure washing and AS 4663 slip-resistance verification for any sealed floor.
  • Recommendations, next periodic window (verified against the contract spec), limitations statement, signature and retained copy.

lessons.title: Step 4 — Routine cleaning
lessons.contentBody:
Routine & periodic hard-floor cleaning

This procedure covers the common school and childcare hard floors — vinyl/PVC, tile and grout, and sealed concrete. It runs the same repeatable sequence every time, because a consistent method gives a consistent finish and makes any problem easy to diagnose. Throughout, dilution and any dwell or drying timings come from the product label, Safety Data Sheet (SDS) and Technical Data Sheet (TDS) — never from memory.

  1. Plan and schedule around the children. Separate routine work from heavier periodic work, schedule around occupancy or into term breaks, prepare a SWMS/JSEA, and confirm Working With Children checks, site induction and access before you start.
  2. Set up SDS, register and PPE. Read a current SDS (within 5 years) for each product, enter it in an accessible hazardous chemicals register (model WHS Reg 346), label every container including decanted ones, fit the PPE the SDS specifies, and confirm mains equipment is AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tagged on a 230 V / 50 Hz, 10 A GPO through an RCD.
  3. Remove dry soil first. Sweep, dust-mop or vacuum thoroughly — most soil on a school floor is dry grit that lifts before any water touches the surface.
  4. Identify the floor and choose a child-safe product. Neutral-pH cleaner protects the finish on vinyl/PVC and sealed concrete for routine work; alkaline chemistry such as Actichem Defender SV (SDS AP672) lifts greasy periodic soil on tile and sealed concrete. Choose the lower-tox option for occupied rooms.
  5. Dilute correctly. Dilute strictly to the label/SDS/TDS ratio — never stronger — using dilution control or metering; dilution yield and coverage (m²/L) are product-specific figures on the TDS. Decant into a labelled container and start ventilating.
  6. Isolate and sign the wet zone. Barrier off and sign the work zone, keep children and pets out, and route cords and hoses clear of walkways.
  7. Apply, dwell and agitate. Apply evenly, dwell per the label, then agitate: flat mop or auto-scrubber for open floor; pre-wet grout and use Dirty Grout Demon brushes for detail and the Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head to clean and recover across open tiled floor.
  8. Treat spots and grout detail. Work spots and stained grout with the right chemistry, testing in an inconspicuous area first and following each spotter's own SDS.
  9. Rinse thoroughly. Flush loosened soil and residue clear of the surface, especially from porous grout lines; an extra rinse pass beats a callback.
  10. Recover and dispose of waste water. Recover the dirty solution by auto-scrubber, wet vacuum or careful pick-up; dispose to sewer per the SDS — never to stormwater or a garden bed.
  11. Dry the floor. Move air with ventilation and RCD-protected air movers, and manage drying by conditions. Do not promise a fixed dry time, and keep the area isolated while damp.
  12. Confirm slip resistance and reinstate. Where a target slip class applies, verify the in-service floor under AS 4663 (not AS 4586, which classifies new surfaces). Remove signage only when the floor is dry, clear and safe.
  13. Document the clean. Record the method per surface, products with current SDS, dilution, equipment test-and-tag status and any slip verification.

Safety

  • As a PCBU, keep a current SDS (within 5 years) for every product, maintain a hazardous chemicals register (Reg 346), and label all containers including decanted ones.
  • Victoria uses its own OHS Act and Regulations — check WorkSafe Victoria; elsewhere use your state regulator (SafeWork NSW, Workplace Health and Safety Queensland).
  • Wear the SDS-specified gloves, eye protection and clothing; take particular care with alkaline cleaners such as Defender SV (AP672).
  • Ventilate throughout; know the SDS first aid and keep the Poisons Information Centre number 13 11 26 to hand.
  • A wet floor is a slip and access hazard — isolate and sign it until dry.
  • Run only current AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tagged mains equipment on a 230 V / 50 Hz, 10 A GPO protected by an RCD/safety switch.

Child-safe practice

  • Select lower-tox products for occupied rooms; reserve stronger chemistry for periodic deep cleans outside child contact.
  • Dilute only to the label ratio to limit hazard and residue where children sit and play.
  • Physically isolate and sign wet floors and keep children and pets out — a barrier beats a sign in childcare.
  • Schedule around occupancy and coordinate re-entry with the centre; ventilate throughout.
  • Keep equipment, cords and chemicals secured and out of reach.
  • This work supports the centre's NQS Quality Area 2 obligations for children's health and safety.

Standards

  • AS 4663 — slip resistance of existing, in-service surfaces (used to verify a cleaned floor against a target class).
  • AS 4586 — slip resistance of new surfaces before installation (distinct from AS 4663).
  • SA HB 198 and the NCC — recommended minimum slip classifications by location; consult directly rather than quoting a rating.
  • AS/NZS 3760 — test-and-tag for mains auto-scrubbers, air movers and chargers.
  • Model WHS Act and Regulations incl. Reg 346 (Safe Work Australia); Victoria's separate OHS framework; GHS-classified SDS.
  • Education and Care Services National Law / NQS Quality Area 2 (ACECQA). ANSI/IICRC S100 is the recognised carpet standard of care only; this course is not IICRC-accredited and awards no IICRC CECs.

Document this

  • SWMS/JSEA for the task.
  • Current SDS for every product in an accessible register (Reg 346).
  • Method statement per surface, with dilution and dwell followed.
  • Working With Children checks and site induction records.
  • AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag records for mains equipment.
  • Any AS 4663 slip-resistance verification records.
  • Cleaning schedule confirmed against the current contract specification.

lessons.title: Step 5 — Sealing
lessons.contentBody:
Applying floor seal to a prepared floor

This procedure covers applying sealer and finish to a floor that is already prepared. Two systems are in scope: an acrylic/polymer seal and finish on resilient floors (vinyl/PVC), and a penetrating/impregnating sealer on tile, grout and concrete. Stripping an old finish, rinsing and neutralising a resilient floor is the separate strip-and-reseal procedure — it is not part of this one. Throughout, dilution, coat count, tack-free/recoat and cure times come from each product's Safety Data Sheet (SDS) and Technical Data Sheet (TDS), never from memory.

  1. Confirm scope. This is seal application to a prepared floor. Confirm which floors and which system from the contract spec, schedule into a deep-clean or holiday window, and confirm SWMS/JSEA, Working With Children checks, induction and access.
  2. Verify the floor is prepared and dry. Confirm it is clean, stripped where needed, neutralised (e.g. with Actichem Pre-Seal / Preseal Pro, SDS AP171) and fully dry. If it is not, complete preparation (or the strip-and-reseal procedure) first.
  3. Select the sealer and read the SDS/TDS. Resilient floors: acrylic/polymer system — Actichem Instaseal (SDS AP420) base then Actichem Instagloss (SDS AP422) finish. Tile/grout/concrete: a penetrating/impregnating sealer — obtain its current SDS and TDS from the supplier; do not guess a code. Choose lower-tox products for a child-occupied facility where the system allows.
  4. Check conditions, coverage and equipment. Confirm temperature, humidity and ventilation are within the TDS range, read the coverage rate (m²/L) from the TDS to plan thin even coats, prepare clean applicators, and confirm mains equipment is AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tagged on a 230 V / 50 Hz, 10 A GPO through an RCD.
  5. Isolate and sign the area. A sealed floor is a slip and chemical-contact hazard through every coat and the full cure. Isolate physically and sign, keep children, staff and pets out, and ventilate throughout.
  6. Apply the first coat — thin and even. Keep a wet edge and avoid puddling. For a penetrating sealer, let it soak in for the TDS time then wipe off excess before it dries so it does not leave a film.
  7. Wait for tack-free / recoat time. Let each coat reach the tack-free point the TDS specifies before the next. Recoating too early traps solvent or water and weakens adhesion. Do not use a fixed time — take it from the TDS.
  8. Apply the remaining coats. Build up the number of thin even coats the TDS specifies — for resilient floors, Instaseal (AP420) base then Instagloss (AP422) finish; for penetrating sealer, wipe excess each coat and avoid film build.
  9. Allow full cure. Tack-free is not cured. Plan the job backwards from the TDS — coats, drying between, then full cure — and keep the area isolated for the whole period before committing a return-to-service date.
  10. Verify slip resistance. After cure, measure the finished in-service floor under AS 4663 to confirm the target slip class (AS 4586 classifies new surfaces only). AS 4663 measures slip resistance — it does not set cure or re-entry times. Set the target class from SA HB 198 and the NCC, not from memory.
  11. Reinstate and hand back. Once fully cured, verified and safe, remove isolation, confirm the surface is clean, coordinate exact re-entry with the site, and provide first-use/maintenance guidance.
  12. Document the job. Record the system per surface, products with SDS/TDS, coverage (m²/L), coat count and the AS 4663 result.

Safety

  • Sealing is applied to a prepared floor; if stripping is still needed, that is the separate strip-and-reseal procedure with its own caustic-chemistry controls.
  • As a PCBU, keep a current SDS (within 5 years) and TDS for every sealer/finish, maintain a hazardous chemicals register (Reg 346), and label all decanted containers.
  • Victoria uses its own OHS Act and Regulations — check WorkSafe Victoria; elsewhere use your state regulator (SafeWork NSW, Workplace Health and Safety Queensland).
  • Wear the SDS-specified PPE, ventilate as directed, know the SDS first aid, and keep the Poisons Information Centre number 13 11 26 to hand.
  • A freshly sealed floor is a slip and chemical-contact hazard for the whole cure — isolate and sign it until fully cured, verified and safe.
  • Never quote a fixed dry, cure, re-entry or recoat time, or a specific slip rating, from memory — use the TDS/SDS and AS 4663 / SA HB 198 / NCC.
  • Run only AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tagged mains equipment on a 230 V / 50 Hz, 10 A GPO protected by an RCD; prepare a SWMS/JSEA.

Child-safe practice

  • Choose lower-tox sealers and finishes for a child-occupied facility where the system allows.
  • Schedule sealing into deep-clean or holiday windows so the area is closed for the full application and cure with no children present.
  • Isolate physically and sign for the whole cure period — a barrier or locked room, not just a sign.
  • Keep children, staff and pets out until the floor is fully cured, slip-verified and handed back; coordinate re-entry with the centre from the TDS.
  • Ventilate throughout to keep fumes away from occupied areas.
  • AS 4663 verification before hand-back protects children in wet and spill-prone areas and supports the centre's NQS Quality Area 2 obligations.

Standards

  • AS 4663 — slip resistance of existing, in-service surfaces (used to re-verify the finished sealed floor).
  • AS 4586 — slip resistance of new surfaces before installation (distinct from AS 4663).
  • SA HB 198 and the NCC — recommended minimum slip classifications by location; consult directly.
  • AS/NZS 3760 — test-and-tag for mains air movers, lighting and equipment.
  • Model WHS Act and Regulations incl. Reg 346 (Safe Work Australia); Victoria's separate OHS framework; GHS-classified SDS.
  • Education and Care Services National Law / NQS Quality Area 2 (ACECQA). Dilution, coats and cure times come from the SDS/TDS; this course is not IICRC-accredited and awards no IICRC CECs.

Document this

  • SWMS/JSEA for the task.
  • Current SDS and TDS for every sealer and finish in an accessible register (Reg 346).
  • Confirmation the floor was prepared, neutralised and fully dry (or that strip-and-reseal was completed first).
  • Method statement per surface: system, coverage (m²/L), coat count and tack-free/cure timings followed from the TDS.
  • Working With Children checks and site induction records.
  • AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag records for mains equipment.
  • AS 4663 slip-resistance verification for the finished floor, with the target class referenced to SA HB 198 / the NCC.
  • Return-to-service date derived from the TDS cure guidance, plus first-use/maintenance guidance provided.

lessons.title: Step 6 — Stripping & sealing
lessons.contentBody:
Service Procedure — Stripping & Sealing

A full strip-and-reseal of a resilient/vinyl/PVC or sealed hard floor is one of the highest-risk jobs in commercial floor care: it uses caustic chemistry, leaves a wet and slippery surface, and depends on getting a sequence of steps right in the right order. This procedure walks the job end to end for a school or childcare site. Every dilution, dwell, coat count and dry/cure/re-entry time is product-specific and comes from the product's Safety Data Sheet (SDS) and Technical Data Sheet (TDS) — never from a fixed rule or from memory.

Step-by-step procedure

  1. Confirm scope, book the window and pre-inspect the floor. Confirm the floor is a resilient/vinyl/PVC or sealed hard floor that is meant to be stripped and resealed, not a bare or no-wax floor that must never be stripped. Book the whole job into a term break, holiday deep-clean or after-hours window. Identify the floor and its condition, measure the area in m², and cut a small test patch in an out-of-the-way spot to confirm the stripper lifts the finish cleanly. On older floors, check the building's asbestos register first: pre-1990s vinyl tiles, sheet-vinyl backing and bituminous adhesives can contain asbestos, and stripping or machine abrasion would disturb it — if asbestos is present or suspected, stop and refer to a licensed assessor rather than abrading.
  2. Prepare documentation and plant before mobilising. Write a SWMS/JSEA for the caustic-stripping task. Build or confirm your hazardous chemicals register with a current SDS (no more than five years old) for the stripper, the prep/neutraliser, the sealer and the finish — for example Actichem Instastrip (SDS AP400) or Power Strip (SDS AP404), Pre-Seal/Preseal Pro (SDS AP171), Instaseal (SDS AP420) and Instagloss (SDS AP422). Read every SDS and TDS and plan the job backwards from them. Check the low-speed rotary and the wet vac each carry a current AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag, and confirm workers hold the correct state or territory Working With Children check plus any site induction.
  3. Move and protect furniture, then isolate and sign the area. Move out furniture and loose items, and protect anything that stays — skirtings, door frames, fixtures and any adjacent carpet — with masking and sheeting so caustic slurry cannot reach them. Hard-barricade the whole work area (barriers or a locked-off room) and put out wet-floor signage. Physical isolation is the control that matters, because children may not read or heed a sign.
  4. Set up ventilation, power and spill control. Open up airflow — windows and fans — to move fumes away from any occupied part of the site, as the SDS directs. Power the rotary and the wet vac from a 230 V / 10 A GPO through an RCD/safety switch, and route leads clear of the wet zone. Set out your spill kit and clean flushing water, and decant the stripper only into a correctly labelled container.
  5. Don PPE and do a final child-safety check. Put on the PPE the stripper's SDS specifies for caustic chemistry — splash goggles, chemical-resistant gloves (nitrile or neoprene), and a chemical-resistant apron and boots. Keep the Poisons Information Centre number (13 11 26) to hand and confirm you know the SDS first-aid steps before you open the drum. Check that no children or staff can enter the area.
  6. Dilute and apply the stripper at the correct dilution and dwell. Dilute the stripper to the ratio on the product label/SDS/TDS — no stronger ‘to be sure’, which only raises the hazard and the residue. Use the TDS coverage rate (m²/L) to work out how much you need. Apply an even, generous film to a section you can realistically strip before it dries, and let it dwell for the time the label specifies. Keep the film wet for the whole dwell, and re-wet if it starts to dry.
  7. Agitate with a low-speed rotary/scrubber and stripping pad. Agitate the dwelled section with a low-speed single-disc rotary/scrubber (typically around 175 rpm, not a high-speed burnisher) fitted with an aggressive stripping pad (black or brown), until the old finish is visibly emulsified. Cut in edges, corners and under toe-kicks by hand with a doodlebug/edging pad or hand brush. Work in manageable sections so no slurry dries back onto the floor.
  8. Pick up the slurry. Recover the emulsified slurry straight away with a wet vac or auto-scrubber before it dries — a dried slurry re-deposits the finish and you start over. Contain the slurry within the barriered zone. Where old finish remains, re-apply stripper, dwell, agitate and re-pick-up rather than leaving patches.
  9. Neutralise rinse and check pH (critical). Flood-rinse the stripped floor with clean water plus a neutraliser/prep such as Actichem Pre-Seal/Preseal Pro (SDS AP171) to remove all stripper residue and bring the surface back to neutral, then recover the rinse. Any alkaline residue left behind will stop the new sealer bonding and can cloud or lift the finish. Check the result with a pH strip or meter: it should read neutral (around pH 7), or the target the prep/finish TDS specifies. Repeat the rinse-and-recover until the pH is neutral and the water comes up clean.
  10. Inspect for residue and residual finish. In good light, look and feel for streaks of leftover finish, cloudy residue or grit — check corners, edges and seam lines where finish hides. Re-strip and re-rinse any patch that is not clean and neutral. Do not seal over residue.
  11. Allow the floor to dry fully. The floor must be completely dry before any sealer goes down — sealing over trapped moisture clouds the finish and wrecks adhesion. Keep airflow moving. Drying time depends on the floor and the conditions and any guidance is in the TDS, so do not commit to a fixed figure. Confirm dryness before sealing.
  12. Apply the first thin coat of seal. Apply the sealer base (for example Actichem Instaseal, SDS AP420) in a thin, even coat — thin coats dry and cure more predictably and build a more durable surface than one thick layer. Cut in the edges first, then lay the body in slightly overlapping strokes, keeping a wet edge. Follow the TDS for coat thickness and the number of seal coats.
  13. Observe tack-free/recoat timing between coats. Recoat only when the previous coat is tack-free — over a coat that is not ready, you trap solvent or water, cloud the finish and weaken adhesion. Test tack-free by touching an inconspicuous edge. The recoat interval is product-specific and comes from the TDS. Apply each further thin coat, alternating direction, letting each reach tack-free first.
  14. Apply the finish/gloss coats and complete the edge-work. Build the presentable, protective surface with the finish/gloss coats (for example Actichem Instagloss, SDS AP422) over the seal, again in thin even coats — edges cut in first, then the body, keeping a wet edge. The number of finish coats and the recoat timing come from the TDS. Between coats, keep the area isolated, signed and free of all foot traffic.
  15. Cure and return-to-service timing. Tack-free is not cured. The finish keeps hardening after it is walkable, and full cure typically takes considerably longer than the recoat interval — the full-cure and re-entry guidance is in the TDS. Plan the job (coats plus drying, then full cure) backwards from the TDS and set the client's return date on that basis. Keep the floor isolated and signed for the whole curing period, and do not drag furniture back over an uncured finish.
  16. Slip-resistance check (AS 4663) and handover. A new finish can behave differently underfoot, so verify slip resistance before handover. Arrange measurement of the finished, in-service floor in accordance with AS 4663 and check it against the target slip class for that location (take the target from the contract, SA HB 198 and the NCC — do not quote a fixed P-rating from memory). AS 4663 measures slip resistance only; it does not set re-entry times. Record the result.
  17. Dispose of slurry to trade-waste and restore the site. Contain and recover all stripping slurry and rinse water and dispose of it to trade-waste under the water authority's approval — never to a stormwater drain, gully, garden or play area. Once the floor is fully cured and slip-tested, remove the barriers, replace the furniture and hand the area back, confirming the reopening with the site.
  18. Record the job and close out. Complete the job record: products used with SDS/TDS codes, dilution and dwell applied, coat counts and the recoat/cure guidance followed, the pH verification for the neutralise rinse, the AS 4663 slip result, the trade-waste disposal reference, and before/after photos.

Safety

  • Strippers are high-alkaline/caustic (GHS corrosive) — read the product SDS (e.g. Actichem Instastrip AP400, Power Strip AP404) before opening; wear splash goggles, chemical-resistant gloves (nitrile/neoprene), apron and boots, and ventilate as the SDS directs.
  • Keep the Poisons Information Centre number (13 11 26) to hand; know the SDS first-aid response for skin, eye, inhalation and ingestion; have clean water ready for flushing.
  • The stripped/wet floor and the freshly sealed floor are serious slip and chemical-contact hazards — hard-barricade and sign the area and keep it isolated for the full wet-and-curing period.
  • Power the rotary and wet vac from a 230 V / 10 A GPO through an RCD/safety switch; use only plant with a current AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag; keep leads out of the wet zone.
  • Older resilient floors (pre-1990s vinyl tiles, sheet-vinyl backing, bituminous adhesive) may contain asbestos — check the site asbestos register before any aggressive stripping or abrasion; if ACM is present or suspected, stop and refer to a licensed assessor. Do not abrade.
  • Decant stripper only into a correctly labelled container; manage caustic slurry and spills per the SDS and never mix product chemistries.

Child-safe

  • Schedule the whole job into a term break, holiday deep-clean or after-hours window — never around children.
  • Physical isolation beats a sign: use hard barriers or a locked-off room, because children may not read or heed signage.
  • Keep the high-alkaline stripper and every chemical secured and out of children's reach; choose lower-tox prep and finish where the job allows and the TDS supports it.
  • Ventilate so fumes are kept away from any occupied part of the site.
  • Do not return the floor to service until it is fully cured AND slip-tested; coordinate the reopening with the centre.
  • Workers hold the correct state or territory Working With Children check plus any site child-safe induction.
  • Contain and recover all slurry and rinse water to trade-waste — never to stormwater, a gully or a garden where children play.

Standards & compliance

  • Model WHS Act & Regulations (Safe Work Australia) as adopted by your state/territory; Victoria works under its own OHS Act & Regulations — check your regulator (SafeWork NSW / WorkSafe Victoria / Workplace Health and Safety Queensland).
  • Hazardous chemicals: current SDS (no more than five years old) for stripper, prep, sealer and finish; hazardous chemicals register (model WHS Regulation 346); correct labelling of decanted containers; GHS classification and pictograms.
  • AS/NZS 3760 in-service electrical test-and-tag on the low-speed rotary and wet vac (230 V / 10 A, RCD-protected).
  • AS 4586:2013 (new pedestrian surfaces — lab/pre-installation) and AS 4663:2013 (existing, in-service surfaces — the correct basis to re-verify the finished floor); recommended minimum classifications per SA HB 198 and the NCC — do not quote a fixed P-rating from memory.
  • National Quality Framework / National Quality Standard Quality Area 2 (children's health and safety), overseen by ACECQA, for childcare settings; state/territory Working With Children checks.
  • Trade-waste approval from the water authority to discharge to sewer; the general environmental duty and state EPA requirements keep contaminated water out of stormwater.
  • Dilution, dwell, coat count, tack-free/recoat, full-cure and re-entry times are product-specific — follow each product's SDS/TDS, not a fixed rule. This is professional-development and contract-readiness training; it is not an IICRC-accredited course and awards no IICRC CECs.

Document this

  • SWMS/JSEA for the caustic-stripping task (and a Construction Induction / White Card if the managed site requires one for access).
  • Hazardous chemicals register with a current SDS for every product on site.
