  • AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag records for the rotary and wet vac.
  • Product and method record: products used with SDS/TDS codes, dilutions applied, dwell used, coat counts, and the manufacturer recoat/cure guidance followed.
  • pH verification record for the neutralise rinse.
  • AS 4663 slip-resistance result for the finished floor against the target class.
  • Trade-waste approval reference and slurry disposal record.
  • Reopening confirmation (fully cured and slip-tested) agreed with the site, plus before/after photos.

lessons.title: Step 7 — Post-care & handover
lessons.contentBody:
Service Procedure — Post Care

Post care is the handover and ongoing-care programme that runs after the job is finished. It protects the result you just delivered by confirming a genuine return-to-service, managing the wet or curing period, timing the client's first routine clean, setting up walk-off matting, and giving the client a written maintenance schedule they can actually follow. Done well, it is often the difference between a one-off job and a renewed contract, because it shows the client you understand how a child-occupied site keeps running after you leave.

  1. Confirm return-to-service readiness. A floor is ready when it is genuinely dry (carpet) or dried and cured (sealed hard floors), not when a clock says so. Confirm against the product SDS and TDS and the conditions on the day, and verify the actual surface before you release it — never hand back on a remembered dry or cure time.
  2. Manage the wet or curing period. Keep the work zone isolated and signed for the whole wet or curing period, relying on physical isolation in child areas rather than a sign alone, and manage drying with airflow while keeping cords and equipment away from children.
  3. Verify slip resistance before handover on sealed hard floors. After the finish has cured per the TDS, arrange AS 4663 measurement (existing, in-service surfaces) against the target slip class agreed with the site by reference to SA HB 198 and the NCC — remembering AS 4663 measures slip resistance only and does not set re-entry times.
  4. Run a formal handover walk-through. Walk the areas with the site contact, point out what was done and which areas are released or still isolated and until when, and confirm who removes barriers and signage. Record the handover with a short signed note or dated photos.
  5. Give first-clean timing guidance for newly sealed floors. A new finish keeps hardening after it is walkable, so advise the client to hold off on wet mopping, scrubbing or harsh chemistry and to resume routine cleaning only once it has properly hardened — with the exact timing and first-clean method taken from the finish TDS, not a fixed number of days.
  6. Set up a walk-off matting programme. Recommend or install walk-off matting at entries and high-traffic transitions to capture grit and moisture before they reach the floor, advise on mat length, regular cleaning or swap-out, and keeping mats flat so they are not a trip hazard.
  7. Hand over a written client maintenance schedule. Split routine work (dry vacuuming, dust mopping, prompt spot and spill attention, neutral-pH damp cleaning) from periodic work (encapsulation or hot water extraction, tile-and-grout deep clean, vinyl strip and seal), map periodic tasks to term breaks and holiday windows, and confirm frequencies against the current contract specification.
  8. Advise on child-safe products for the client's own cleaning. Recommend lower-tox products suited to occupied spaces, diluted strictly per label, with a current SDS held in an accessible register and correct labelling of any decanted container — supporting NQS Quality Area 2.
  9. Leave a wet-floor management protocol. Give the client a short protocol for spills and routine mopping (prompt attention, wet-floor signage, keep children clear until dry), and remind them any client-run pressure-wash run-off must be kept out of stormwater and directed to sewer under a trade-waste agreement.
  10. Hand over the documentation pack. Provide the SDS register, method statements, completion report with before-and-after evidence, any AS 4663 slip-resistance records, and the written maintenance schedule and first-clean guidance.
  11. Book the next periodic service into a low-occupancy window. Schedule the next strip and seal or carpet extraction into a term break, holiday or deep-clean window with uninterrupted access, ventilation and drying time, and confirm dates and frequencies against the contract spec.
  12. Set a feedback and re-inspection trigger. Agree a check-in after the first routine cleaning cycle, a defined re-inspection point, and a clear contact for concerns — using feedback and re-inspection to tune the maintenance schedule over time.

Safety

  • A floor stays a slip and access hazard until genuinely dry or cured; keep it isolated and signed and confirm readiness against the SDS/TDS, never a remembered time.
  • In child-occupied areas rely on physical isolation (barriers, locked rooms, out-of-hours scheduling), not signage alone; manage drying with airflow and keep cords clear.
  • Leave a wet-floor protocol for spills and routine mopping, and keep any client-run pressure-wash run-off out of stormwater (trade-waste to sewer).
  • Reinforce label-rate dilution and correct labelling of decanted containers for the client's own cleaning. Poisons Information is 13 11 26.

Child-safe

  • Hold physical isolation of wet or curing floors until genuinely ready — children may not read or heed a sign.
  • Advise lower-tox products suited to occupied spaces, diluted per label, with a current SDS held for each.
  • Schedule periodic deep treatments into term breaks or holiday windows so heavy chemistry and drying happen away from children.
  • Keep walk-off mats flat and maintained so matting reduces tracked-in soil without becoming a trip hazard; frame the programme as supporting NQS Quality Area 2.

Standards

  • Product SDS/TDS — the only correct source for dry-between-coats, full-cure, re-entry and first-clean timing; do not quote a fixed time.
  • AS 4663 (existing/in-service surfaces) for verifying a sealed floor; AS 4586 (new surfaces) noted for contrast; SA HB 198 and the NCC for recommended slip classes.
  • Model WHS Regulations (current SDS within 5 years, Reg 346 register, labelling of decanted containers; Victoria under its own OHS Act); state water-authority trade-waste and state EPA duties for client-run pressure washing; AS/NZS 3760 for equipment left or used on site.
  • ANSI/IICRC S100 as a recognised standard of care only — this service is NOT IICRC-accredited and awards no CECs; NQF / NQS Quality Area 2 (ACECQA) and the current contract spec govern maintenance frequencies.

Document this

  • Return-to-service confirmation referencing the SDS/TDS and on-the-day conditions, plus a handover record (signed note or dated photos) showing areas released or still isolated.
  • AS 4663 slip-resistance verification for any sealed floor, checked against the agreed target class.
  • Written client maintenance schedule (routine vs periodic) and first-clean guidance deferred to the finish TDS.
  • Walk-off matting recommendation and child-safe product guidance for the client's own cleaning (low-tox, label-rate dilution, SDS register, labelling).
  • Wet-floor and run-off / trade-waste guidance for client-run pressure washing.
  • Documentation pack handed over (SDS register, method statements, completion report, slip records, schedule) and the next periodic booking plus feedback / re-inspection trigger.

lessons.title: Scheduling around occupancy, term breaks and deep-clean windows
lessons.contentBody:
Schools and childcare centres are occupied facilities, so the schedule is part of the service, not an afterthought. The work splits into two kinds. Daily or routine work is the light, frequent cleaning that keeps a floor presentable and safe between heavier treatments. Periodic work is the heavy, intermittent work: hard-floor strip and seal, and carpet hot water extraction. These periodic jobs are typically scheduled into deep-clean or school-holiday windows rather than squeezed into a daily routine. That is the directional pattern in published programs such as those from School Infrastructure NSW, but the actual frequency and timing always sit in the current contract specification, so verify against the spec rather than assuming.

The reason periodic work goes into holiday or after-hours windows is simple. Stripping and sealing a vinyl floor leaves it wet and curing, which is a slip and access hazard, and the floor is out of action until the product's own drying steps are complete. Carpet extraction adds moisture that needs time to dry before the space is reused. You cannot reliably run either through a corridor full of children. Choosing a low-occupancy window removes that conflict.

When you do have to work in an occupied facility, plan for low disruption with three levers.

  • Sequencing: work room by room or zone by zone so the centre always has usable space. Treat one area, let it return to service, then move to the next, rather than closing everything at once.
  • Drying: build realistic drying time into the plan. For carpet, encapsulation is a low-moisture method that returns a space to use faster than hot water extraction, which removes more soil but leaves more moisture. That is a genuine dry-time versus soil-load trade-off you weigh against the window you have. Do not quote a fixed dry time; it depends on the method, the conditions and the product.
  • Signage and isolation: barrier off and sign wet or curing floors. A freshly stripped or recoated floor is a slip hazard while wet, so isolate it and keep foot traffic out until it is ready.

Term breaks and school holidays are your main periodic-work windows. They give you uninterrupted access, room to ventilate caustic strippers properly, and enough time for multiple thin sealer coats with drying between each coat. They also let you re-verify slip resistance on a finished hard floor before students return. Map your periodic program to these windows early, and confirm dates and access with the site so you are not relying on assumed frequencies.

A practical planning sequence looks like this. First, separate the routine scope from the periodic scope in the contract. Second, place each periodic task in the appropriate deep-clean or holiday window. Third, for any work that must happen while the facility is occupied, write the sequence, the expected return-to-service approach, and the signage and isolation plan. Fourth, confirm the plan against the current contract spec and the site's own rules before you commit dates.

Treating scheduling this way protects the people in the building, keeps the floor safe and serviceable, and shows the client you understand how a child-occupied site actually runs. It is often the difference between a contract that is renewed and one that is not.

lessons.title: Compliance & documentation: SDS register, WWC checks, NQS QA2 and school cleaning standards
lessons.contentBody:
A school or childcare contract assumes a baseline of paperwork before you set foot on site. This lesson maps that baseline at a high level and points you to the authority for each item. None of it replaces the contract's own requirements or the site's own rules; treat these as starting points, not a complete compliance list.

SDS register. If you use, handle or store hazardous chemicals, you are a PCBU with duties under the relevant work health and safety law. You must obtain and keep a current safety data sheet for each hazardous chemical and keep them accessible to workers at the workplace. A safety data sheet is treated as current when it is not more than five years old, and each sheet states its last-reviewed date. You also maintain a hazardous chemicals register with a current SDS for each chemical. Note that VIC operates under its own OHS framework rather than the model WHS laws, so confirm with your state regulator. Direct questions to SafeWork Australia and your state body.

Working With Children checks. Child-related work, which includes contractors at schools and childcare centres, generally requires a Working With Children check. These are legislated and administered separately in each state and territory, and the names differ: NSW has the WWCC, VIC has the Working with Children Check, QLD has the Blue Card, and the ACT uses WWVP registration. They are generally not auto-transferable between jurisdictions, so a check from one state may not be recognised in another. Just as importantly, a WWC check may not be the only screening a site requires; many sites add their own child-safe induction. Do not treat it as sufficient on its own, and check the exact eligibility and process with the relevant state or territory body rather than relying on a general summary.

Childcare NQS Quality Area 2. Childcare operates under the National Quality Framework, overseen by ACECQA. Quality Area 2 covers children's health and safety, including minimising risk and protecting children from harm and infection, across Standard 2.1 (health) and Standard 2.2 (safety). Your floor-care methods, product choices and scheduling should align with that intent. This is the centre's obligation; your job is to support it, not to certify the centre against any standard. Point to ACECQA for the detail.

School cleaning standards. Public schools are cleaned to published cleaning performance standards. In NSW, for example, public schools are cleaned under a whole-of-government cleaning contract with published performance standards from School Infrastructure NSW. Frequencies and tasks in those standards are directional for your planning; the binding detail is the current contract specification, so verify against it.

A simple readiness check before you quote:

  • Is there a current SDS for every chemical you will bring, all within five years and in an accessible register?
  • Does each worker on site hold the correct state or territory WWC check, and have you confirmed any additional site induction?
  • Do your methods and products support, not undermine, NQS Quality Area 2 in a childcare setting?
  • Have you read the actual school cleaning standard or contract spec rather than assuming the frequencies?

Getting this baseline in order signals to a client that you can be trusted on a child-occupied site, which is exactly what the contract is buying.

lessons.title: Capstone: build your child-safe floor-care service offer
lessons.contentBody:
This capstone pulls the whole course into one deliverable: a one-page, child-safe floor-care service offer you can put to a school or childcare client. The aim is not a marketing brochure. It is a clear statement of how you will deliver safe, compliant work in a child-occupied facility, backed by the documentation a contract expects. Build it section by section.

Methods per surface. State your approach for each surface you will service. For carpet, name the method and why: hot water extraction for deeper soil removal, or encapsulation as a low-moisture option that returns a space to use faster in an occupied site. For tile and grout, describe your cleaning approach. For vinyl and resilient floors, set out the strip and seal process at a high level: strip, rinse and neutralise, dry, then multiple thin sealer and finish coats with drying between coats. Make clear that dilution ratios, coat counts and any drying or re-entry steps come from each product's SDS and technical data sheet, not from a fixed rule.

SDS register. List the chemicals you will bring and confirm a current SDS, within five years, for each one, held in an accessible register. Where a product lacks a current SDS, state that you will obtain the current SDS from the supplier before use.

Child-safe product choices. Explain how your product and method choices support NQS Quality Area 2 in childcare settings, including minimising risk and infection. Keep this as alignment of intent, not a certification claim.

Slip-resistance verification. Commit to re-verifying slip resistance on finished hard floors after sealing, on the basis of AS 4663, which measures existing in-service surfaces. Be clear that AS 4663 classifies and measures only; it does not set cure or re-entry times, and you should not quote a specific P-rating requirement without checking HB 198 or the NCC.

Run-off control for pressure washing. State how you will keep wash water out of stormwater, because stormwater flows untreated to waterways. Confine the area, collect the wastewater and direct it to sewer, and note that discharging trade wastewater to sewer needs prior written approval through a trade-waste agreement with the water authority. VIC operators should note EPA Victoria treats contaminated wash water as industrial waste.

Scheduling. Show that periodic strip and seal and carpet extraction sit in deep-clean or holiday windows, with low-disruption sequencing, realistic drying and signage and isolation for any work done while the facility is occupied. Confirm frequencies against the current contract spec.

Documentation. List what you will provide: the SDS register, the correct state or territory WWC checks for your workers plus any site induction, your method statements, and your slip-resistance verification records.

Pull these onto a single page. A short readiness checklist helps:

  • Methods named per surface, with product steps deferred to SDS and TDS.
  • Current SDS register in place.
  • Child-safe product rationale aligned to NQS QA2.
  • AS 4663 slip verification after sealing.
  • Run-off captured to sewer under a trade-waste agreement.
  • Periodic work scheduled into low-occupancy windows.
  • WWC checks and documentation confirmed.

Then take the single next action this whole course has been driving toward: put a compliant, child-safe floor-care offer to a school or childcare client.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: the contract
lessons.contentBody:
Test yourself on winning and servicing a school or childcare floor-care contract. Choose one answer for each question, then check the answers below.

1. Periodic work such as hard-floor strip and seal or carpet hot water extraction is typically scheduled into:

  • A. The daily routine clean
  • B. Deep-clean or school-holiday windows
  • C. Peak occupancy, to show the client the work
  • D. Whenever a worker has a spare hour

2. For a current safety data sheet, which statement is correct?

  • A. It must be reprinted every week
  • B. It is treated as current if not more than five years old, with a stated last-reviewed date
  • C. It only needs to be kept at head office, not on site
  • D. It is optional if the chemical seems mild

3. Working With Children checks in Australia are:

  • A. A single national check valid everywhere
  • B. Issued only to permanent school staff, never contractors
  • C. State and territory based, generally not auto-transferable, and not necessarily the only screening a site requires
  • D. Replaced entirely by a police check

4. After sealing a hard floor, slip resistance is best re-verified on the basis of:

  • A. AS 4586, which classifies new surfaces before installation
  • B. AS 4663, which measures existing in-service surfaces
  • C. The sealer's gloss level
  • D. The SDS re-entry time

5. Pressure-washing run-off should be:

  • A. Hosed straight into the nearest stormwater drain
  • B. Left to evaporate on the surface
  • C. Confined and collected, then directed to sewer under a trade-waste agreement
  • D. Tipped into a garden bed

Answers

  • 1. B — Strip and seal and extraction leave floors wet or curing, so they go into low-occupancy deep-clean or holiday windows; verify timing against the contract spec.
  • 2. B — A safety data sheet is treated as current when not more than five years old and states its last-reviewed date, and it must be accessible to workers on site.
  • 3. C — WWC checks are administered per state and territory, are generally not auto-transferable, and a site may also require its own child-safe induction.
  • 4. B — AS 4663 measures existing surfaces in service, so it is the correct basis to re-verify a finished sealed floor; it measures only and does not set re-entry times.
  • 5. C — Stormwater flows untreated to waterways, so run-off must be confined, collected and directed to sewer under prior trade-waste approval.

courses.title: CARSI Maintenance Company Onboarding — Professional Floor Care & Operational Readiness
courses.description:
Part of CARSI Maintenance Company Onboarding. The internal induction for CARSI Maintenance Company floor-care technicians, supervisors and facilities staff who deliver cleaning, polishing, sealing, maintenance and hygiene services across schools, childcare, education, public buildings, commercial and high-traffic facilities.

Twelve modules take a new starter from induction to independent operator: what professional floor care is and why it matters, health and hygiene in child-sensitive environments, WHS/PPE/SDS and chemical safety, surface identification, methods, machinery, vehicle and equipment readiness, site setup and hazard control, professional conduct, confidentiality and CCTV awareness, job completion and evidence, and a final practical assessment. Each module ends in an auto-marked knowledge check. Written in Australian English; training supports good judgement and does not replace the SDS/TDS, standards, or a site’s own rules.
courses.shortDescription: CARSI Maintenance Company Onboarding — the floor-care induction for technicians, supervisors and facilities staff: safety, hygiene, methods, operational readiness and professional conduct across sensitive sites.

meta.program: Professional Floor Care Onboarding & Operational Readiness Program

pricing.note: Organisation subscription: AUD $1,295/month + GST, unlimited students. Provisioned manually; recurring billing is not yet wired (Stripe subscription build is separate).

modules.title: Module 1: Introduction to Professional Floor Care

lessons.title: What professional floor care is
lessons.contentBody:
Welcome. This program is for technicians, supervisors and facilities staff who deliver floor surface cleaning, polishing, sealing, maintenance and hygiene services across schools, childcare, education and public buildings, commercial sites and other high-traffic facilities.

Professional floor care is more than cleaning. It is a safety, hygiene, presentation, asset-protection and operational-readiness service. There are five service types you will hear about:

  • Routine cleaning — regular removal of soil and spills.
  • Deep / restorative cleaning — periodic, heavier soil removal.
  • Polishing / burnishing — restoring finish and appearance.
  • Sealing / coating — protecting the surface.
  • Maintenance — keeping the result so the next visit is easier.

We use careful language about what floor care does. It supports hygiene, reduces soil load and helps manage contaminants. We do not make medical claims and we do not claim to sterilise.

lessons.title: Why floors matter in sensitive facilities
lessons.contentBody:
Floors are high-contact, high-risk surfaces. Everyone walks on them, and they carry tracked-in soil, spills and contaminants. In schools, childcare and public buildings, children, staff and vulnerable people use those floors at close range.

How we clean and maintain a floor affects:

  • hygiene and the soil load underfoot;
  • slip, trip and fall risk;
  • the presentation of the facility and the client’s confidence in us;
  • the life of the floor — an expensive asset we protect or damage.

Poor floor care does the opposite: it spreads soil, creates slip hazards, looks bad, and shortens the life of the surface. That is why the standard matters on every job.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: introduction
The contaminants we help manage
lessons.contentBody:
Floor care helps manage a range of contaminants that build up or get tracked in:

  • pollen, dust and dirt;
  • body soils, food soils and spills;
  • tracked-in contaminants from outside;
  • conditions that support mould growth when moisture is left behind.

Our job is to remove and reduce that load with the correct process — not to spread it around. Dirty pads, mops and cleaning solution re-deposit soil, so clean equipment and the right method matter as much as the chemical.

Careful language again: we support a facility’s hygiene and help manage contaminants. We do not make disinfection or health-outcome claims.

lessons.title: Cross-contamination control and child-sensitive work
lessons.contentBody:
Two habits prevent cross-contamination:

  • work clean-to-dirty — from cleaner areas toward dirtier ones;
  • keep colour-coded equipment separated, especially toilet and kitchen tools from general-area tools.

In child-occupied and sensitive spaces, adjust the job:

  • choose lower-tox products where suitable and read the SDS;
  • ventilate the room when using chemicals;
  • isolate and sign wet areas, and keep chemicals away from children;
  • time the work around occupancy where you can.

Infection-control awareness means we support the facility’s hygiene practices — we do not claim to disinfect or guarantee an outcome.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: health and hygiene

modules.title: Module 3: WHS, PPE, SDS & Chemical Safety

lessons.title: Reading a Safety Data Sheet (SDS)
lessons.contentBody:
Our chemicals include strippers and acids/alkalis that can burn skin and eyes and give off fumes. The Safety Data Sheet tells you how to use them safely — it is not optional reading.

Under the model WHS framework, a workplace must keep current SDS (not more than five years old) and a hazardous chemicals register, accessible to workers. The sections you use most on the job are: hazards, PPE, first aid, handling and storage, and spill response.

Jurisdiction flag: most states follow the model WHS framework, but Victoria operates under its own OHS Act and Regulations. Check the rules for the state you are working in.

lessons.title: PPE and the hierarchy of controls
lessons.contentBody:
PPE is the last resort in the hierarchy of controls — used after elimination, substitution, isolation and engineering controls have been considered. PPE supplements those higher controls; it does not replace them.

Select your PPE from the product SDS — it specifies gloves, eye protection and respiratory protection for that chemical. Check PPE condition before use and carry spares.

Ventilation matters too: a workplace must keep airborne contaminants below the relevant exposure standard. The SDS carries that exposure information — read it to judge whether you need extra ventilation, especially in occupied or child-occupied rooms.

lessons.title: Labelling, storage and spills
lessons.contentBody:
Labelling: every container must be labelled, including decanted ones — on the body of the container, clearly visible. The only exception is a chemical used immediately after decanting where the container is then cleaned out. If a decant is left for later or for another worker, it must be labelled.

Storage: segregate incompatible chemicals (acids vs alkalis, oxidisers) per the SDS storage advice — on the vehicle and on site.

Spills: keep a spill kit available; the SDS gives spill and disposal advice. Contain, clean up and dispose per the SDS and the site emergency plan.

The “never” list: no unlabelled bottles; never mix chemicals; never leave a decant for another worker unlabelled.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: WHS, PPE and SDS
Recognising the common surfaces
lessons.contentBody:
The fastest way to cause expensive damage is to use the wrong chemical or pad on a surface. Start every job by identifying what you are working on. Common surfaces:

  • vinyl, VCT and linoleum;
  • rubber;
  • concrete (sealed or unsealed);
  • terrazzo;
  • ceramic and porcelain tile;
  • natural stone;
  • timber and engineered timber;
  • epoxy / resin;
  • carpet and carpet tiles.

See the full surface guide in the onboarding pack (standards/floor-surface-identification.md) for how to recognise each one and the safe and unsafe chemistry for it.

lessons.title: Porosity, sealed vs unsealed, and pH
lessons.contentBody:
Two properties decide how you treat a floor:

  • Porosity — porous, semi-porous or non-porous. Porous surfaces absorb, so dwell time and chemical choice change.
  • Sealed vs unsealed — always confirm whether a surface (e.g. vinyl or concrete) is sealed before stripping or applying aggressive chemistry.

Basic chemistry: cleaners are neutral, alkaline or acidic; plus solvents, strippers, sealers and polishes. Each belongs in particular places. Two rules to remember:

  • Never use a strong acidic cleaner on natural stone — it etches.
  • Keep water and strippers off untreated timber — it is water/stripper sensitive.

Always test a small, inconspicuous area first. Cure, dilution and slip-rating figures come from the SDS/TDS and the relevant standard — not from guesswork.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: surface identification

modules.title: Module 5: Cleaning, Polishing, Sealing & Maintenance Methods

lessons.title: Why method discipline matters
lessons.contentBody:
Method discipline is what makes the result consistent. Each step does a job:

  • Dwell time — lets the chemical work;
  • Agitation — breaks the soil free;
  • Extraction — removes soil and solution;
  • Rinsing / neutralising — removes residue so the floor does not re-soil or stay slippery;
  • Polishing / sealing — finishes and protects.

Skipping dwell, rinsing or neutralising is what causes streaking, residue, fast re-soiling and slip problems.

lessons.title: Carpet: extraction vs encapsulation
lessons.contentBody:
Two common carpet methods, and a trade-off:

  • Hot water extraction (HWE) — deeper soil removal, but longer dry time.
  • Encapsulation — low-moisture, faster return to service — useful in occupied rooms.

In an occupied school or care room, the dry-time vs soil-removal trade-off often points to encapsulation so the space comes back into use quickly. Do not state fixed dry times — they depend on the product and conditions.

lessons.title: Hard floors, strip & seal, and slip resistance
lessons.contentBody:
Hard-floor cleaning (including tile and grout) needs the right dwell, agitation and rinse, then a check on slip resistance after cleaning or sealing.

Vinyl strip-and-seal, end to end: strip → rinse/neutralise → dry → thin sealer/finish coats. Strippers are typically alkaline/caustic — ventilation and PPE are required, and a wet or curing floor is a slip/access hazard, so isolate and sign it.

Cure, re-entry and dilution come from the SDS/TDS, not guesswork. Slip-resistance targets come from the relevant standard (AS 4663 / HB 198 / NCC). After sealing, verify the finished floor against the target class.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: methods
The machines and what they do
lessons.contentBody:
Floor-care machines are powerful and run on power or batteries near water and people. Know what each does and when to use it:

  • Rotary machine — scrubbing, stripping, polishing (pad/brush dependent);
  • Auto scrubber — scrub and recover in one pass;
  • Burnisher — high-speed finish/polish;
  • Wet vacuum / extractor — recover water and solution;
  • Pressure system — hard outdoor surfaces (with run-off control).

Run a pre-use check before any machine touches the floor.

lessons.title: Pads, brushes and safe operation
lessons.contentBody:
Pad and brush selection changes the result — an aggressive pad on a soft finish causes damage, the wrong brush on grout wastes effort.

Electrical and trip safety:

  • Portable equipment (floor polishers, extension leads) needs RCD / safety-switch protection; operate a portable RCD’s test button each time you plug in.
  • Check leads for damage on the pre-start; a test-and-tag program (referencing AS/NZS 3760) is how we show equipment is maintained — intervals vary by state.
  • Manage leads and hoses so they are not trip hazards, especially across paths children use.

Any unsafe act in operation is an automatic fail in assessment. When in doubt, stop.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: machinery operation
If it is not checked, it can stop the job
lessons.contentBody:
A job does not fail only because of poor cleaning. It fails because the vehicle is not ready, the TruckMount cannot get to site, a machine will not start, the right tool was left behind, PPE or chemical is missing, a battery is flat, or a hose is damaged.

If it is not checked, it can stop the job. That is why the daily pre-start, completed and signed before you leave, is mandatory. It covers the vehicle, TruckMount (where fitted), machines, batteries, hoses and leads, tools, pads/brushes, PPE, signage and barriers, chemical stock, spill kit, first-aid kit and fire extinguisher.

Carry the standard spares — spare pads, fuses, hose fittings and key chemicals — so a small fault does not stop a whole job.

lessons.title: Daily, weekly and monthly maintenance, and fault reporting
lessons.contentBody:
Keep equipment ready on three cycles:

  • Daily — post-use clean-down, empty/rinse tanks, inspect pads/brushes/hoses/leads, charge batteries.
  • Weekly — deeper clean, filters, wear checks, hose fittings, lead/RCD inspection, lubrication per the manual.
  • Monthly — service items, tool-inventory reconciliation, restock signage/first-aid/spill kit, fire-extinguisher check, review electrical test-and-tag status.

Fault reporting: log the fault, tell the right person, and tag faulty equipment out of service — do not keep using it. A machine that fails its pre-start does not go to site.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: maintenance
Arrival, sign-in and the job briefing
lessons.contentBody:
Most incidents and complaints come from the setup, not the cleaning. Run the arrival sequence every time:

  • park and unload per the site’s rules;
  • complete site sign-in;
  • make contact with the site contact;
  • take the job briefing — scope, access, occupancy, and water and power access.

Confirm you can physically get equipment to the work area before you start unloading everything.

lessons.title: Inspection, risk assessment and work-zone control
lessons.contentBody:
Before you start: inspect the surface and do an on-the-spot risk assessment — hazards, occupancy, children present, and your wet-floor plan.

Then set up a controlled work zone:

  • put up wet-floor signage and barricade / exclude the area before the floor is wet;
  • keep exits and pedestrian paths clear;
  • route and manage leads and hoses so they are not trip hazards;
  • ventilate occupied or child-occupied rooms when using chemicals;
  • place chemicals where they are secure and never within reach of children.

Remember: signage is an administrative control that supplements separating people from wet areas — it is not the primary control.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: site setup
The professionalism standard
lessons.contentBody:
On a school or childcare site, how we behave is as visible as how we clean. Professional conduct is what earns the trust that keeps the contract. The standard covers:

  • company uniform, personal hygiene and presentation;
  • behaviour and language on site;
  • respect for children, staff, teachers, visitors, tenants and facility users;
  • vehicle cleanliness and where you park;
  • where you unload, where chemicals go, and how hoses and leads are run.

See the full professionalism standard in the onboarding pack (standards/professionalism-conduct.md).

lessons.title: Presentation, communication and trust
lessons.contentBody:
Control your work zone so it does not disrupt site operations — classes, drop-off, deliveries. If a teacher needs you to move for a class, respond politely and adjust.

Communicate delays, risks and issues early and plainly. A site contact who hears about a delay early stays a happy client; one who finds out late does not.

Professionalism is not extra — it is the difference between a job we did and a contract we keep.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: professional conduct
Confidentiality and the photo rule
lessons.contentBody:
We work inside other people’s facilities, often around children and private information. One photo of a screen or a child, or one careless comment, can breach trust and the law.

Images of an identifiable person are personal information under the Privacy Act 1988, collected only where reasonably necessary. So the default is clear:

  • do not discuss private site information;
  • do not photograph people, children, documents, screens or private information;
  • when you need a before/after photo, frame out people and private information — if you cannot, record why in the notes instead.

lessons.title: Security, restricted areas and assume-CCTV conduct
lessons.contentBody:
Keep work zones clean, controlled and secure. Do not prop or block secure doors. Respect restricted areas. On child-occupied sites, follow child-safe conduct — never be alone with a child. A Working With Children check is a starting point, not the whole obligation; it sits alongside the child-safe code of conduct.

Assume CCTV is operating. Behave as though every action is visible to the client. That single habit protects you and the company’s reputation.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: confidentiality and security
Final inspection, pack-down and evidence
lessons.contentBody:
If we cannot show what we did, we cannot defend the work or get paid cleanly. Finish every job properly:

  • final inspection against the job scope;
  • pack-down — equipment, leads, hoses and signage, and leave the area clean;
  • photo evidence within the confidentiality rules;
  • clear, complete completion notes.

If you find pre-existing damage during the final inspection, photograph and record it so it is not wrongly attributed to us.

lessons.title: Defect reporting, escalation and handover
lessons.contentBody:
Report defects and anything outside the standard, and follow the escalation process for anything you cannot resolve on site (unexpected floor condition, equipment failure, access problem, confidentiality conflict).

Then hand over to the client/site contact and get sign-off where required. A clean handover is what turns a finished job into a renewed contract.

Escalation contacts and timeframes are set by the company — know yours before you go solo.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: completion and handover
What the final assessment covers
lessons.contentBody:
This is the gate to working solo. It proves the standard holds when no one is guiding each step. You will run a full standard job, end to end, assessed by a supervisor against the checklists and standards:

  • vehicle and equipment readiness;
  • site arrival, sign-in, briefing and setup;
  • surface identification and the correct method and chemical;
  • safe machine operation and hazard control;
  • professional conduct and confidentiality/security;
  • close-out with evidence and a clear handover.

Pass: at least 85% overall and 100% on every safety-critical and reputation-critical item — no automatic-fail acts.

lessons.title: Scenario walk-throughs
lessons.contentBody:
Be ready to talk through judgement, not just steps:

  • Mid-job you find the surface is not what the scope said. What do you change, and who do you tell? (Stop before damage, document, confirm a revised method or re-scope.)
  • A flat battery stops your scrubber halfway. What is your recovery and escalation? (Switch to a backup if in scope; otherwise notify, complete what is safe, re-book.)
  • You need a photo but private information is in shot. What do you do? (Frame it out or record why no photo was taken.)

Knowing when to stop and escalate is part of passing.

lessons.title: Independent-operator clearance
lessons.contentBody:
When you pass Module 12 and all 12 modules are signed off, your supervisor signs the independent-operator clearance — you are cleared to run standard jobs on your own, with named escalation paths for anything outside the standard.

Independence is the start of the standard, not the end of development. Expect refreshers on the safety-critical modules and ongoing toolbox talks drawn from real jobs.

There is no time pressure to pass. Competence comes before the calendar — the clearance is signed when the work earns it.

courses.title: Australian H5 Bird Flu Awareness for Restoration, IAQ and Facility Professionals
courses.description:
This course helps Australian restoration, cleaning, indoor air quality, facility, strata, insurance-support and professional remediation teams communicate calmly, report correctly, protect workers, stay within scope, document field work and avoid unsupported claims about H5 bird flu.

It is for professionals who may receive questions about sick or dead birds, field documentation, building contamination concerns or client communication during the current Australian H5 bird flu detections in migratory seabirds.

This course is not veterinary training, wildlife handling training, public-health diagnosis, government biosecurity response training or product endorsement training. It follows the official Australian Government and Australian Centre for Disease Control reporting pathway: do not touch sick or dead birds, record what you see and report to the Emergency Animal Disease Hotline on 1800 675 888.
courses.shortDescription: Report correctly, protect workers, document field work and communicate calmly about H5 bird flu — professional-boundary awareness training for Australian restoration, IAQ and facility teams.

meta.officialSourceRecheck: 2026-07-05: DAFF/Australian CDC/Animal Health Australia guidance reconfirmed current — migratory seabird detections only (WA, SA), no poultry, public risk low, hotline 1800 675 888 correct. Recheck again before publish per lms-import-plan.md gate 1.

lessons.title: Official message and reporting pathway — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Australia has reported H5 bird flu detections in migratory seabirds. The professional communication position is calm and evidence-led: follow Australian Government and Australian Centre for Disease Control (CDC) advice, avoid speculation and direct the public to the correct reporting pathway.

If a sick or dead bird or animal is found, the public message is simple:

  1. Do not touch it.
  2. Avoid the area.
  3. Keep children and pets away.
  4. Record the location and what is seen.
  5. Take photos or video only if safe.
  6. Report to the Emergency Animal Disease Hotline on 1800 675 888.
Report first. Do not touch. Record the location. Call 1800 675 888.

Professional caution: Do not tell the public that a site is infected or safe. Use neutral language: observed, reported, excluded from scope, referred to authority.

lessons.title: Professional role, scope and boundaries — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Professional restoration and IAQ members may be asked to assist property owners, facility managers, strata managers, insurers or contractors. The role is support, not authority replacement.

Professionals can assist with:

  • site notes and photo records
  • access control and exclusion-zone records
  • worker-safety planning
  • PPE record keeping
  • cleaning records
  • product-label and SDS record checks
  • client communication notes
  • RestoreAssist.app reporting
  • escalation to the correct authority pathway

Professionals must not:

  • handle wildlife without authorisation
  • diagnose avian influenza
  • certify a site as disease-free
  • override government, veterinary, wildlife or public-health authorities
  • claim that a product or dry-fogging method is required unless written authority direction and product evidence support that claim
Our role is to document, protect workers and communicate clearly. Official disease response sits with the relevant authorities.

modules.title: Worker safety, PPE awareness and clean/dirty zones

lessons.title: Worker safety, PPE awareness and clean/dirty zones — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Before any site work, complete a site-specific WHS risk assessment. Determine what has been observed, whether authorities have been contacted, whether the site is under direction and whether the work is within the contractor's scope.

Use clean and dirty zones to reduce confusion and cross-contamination risk. This course does not prescribe a universal PPE set because PPE must be matched to task, risk, authority guidance, product label and SDS. However, records should show what PPE was selected, who wore it, whether there were breaches and what waste or equipment movement was excluded from the job.

Documentation fields:

  • Who attended
  • What was observed
  • Whether sick or dead birds or animals were present
  • Whether the hotline or authority pathway was used
  • PPE selected
  • Access restrictions
  • Client instructions
  • Work not performed
  • Photos and timestamps
If it is not documented, it cannot be reviewed.

lessons.title: Cleaning-before-disinfection and product-claim limits — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
CARSI's training position is simple: clean first, disinfect second, document third. Disinfection claims must never exceed the product label, SDS, regulator position or evidence base.

Hydrogen-peroxide dry fogging, including Halosil or HaloFogger-style systems, must not be described as required by Australian Government H5 control measures unless a written authority direction and product evidence support that claim. This course may discuss dry fogging only as a possible adjunct method where legal, label-supported, risk-assessed, unoccupied where required, and performed by trained operators after source removal and cleaning.

NeoSan or any other product training must remain label-led and SDS-led until approved H5 or relevant organism claims are verified.

Product training is not product hype. Use the label, SDS, evidence and authority direction.

lessons.title: RestoreAssist.app documentation pathway — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
RestoreAssist.app is the documentation pathway for campaign field records. A professional file should allow a client, insurer, supervisor or authority to understand what was observed, what was reported, what was done, what was not done and what evidence supported the decision.

Suggested record sections:

  • intake notes
  • site address and contact
  • observation notes
  • authority/reporting record
  • photos and captions
  • worker attendance
  • PPE record
  • access control
  • cleaning record
  • product and SDS record
  • exclusion and limitation notes
  • client communication
  • completion statement
RestoreAssist turns the job from a memory into a defensible record.

lessons.title: Client, media and social communication — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
This campaign is a public-interest and professional-readiness campaign. The tone must be measured. Do not use terms such as crisis, outbreak or emergency unless those are the exact words used by authorities for the current situation.

Use approved wording:

  • We follow Australian Government advice.
  • Do not touch sick or dead birds.
  • Report to 1800 675 888.
  • Our role is documentation, worker safety and professional support.
  • We do not replace government, veterinary, wildlife or public-health authorities.
  • Product use must follow label, SDS and authority requirements.

Avoid:

  • fear-based headlines
  • unsupported claims
  • fake statistics
  • fake expert quotes
  • fake portraits
  • unapproved personal contact details
  • product claims that exceed evidence
Calm language builds trust. Panic language damages credibility.

lessons.title: Professional-boundary statement
lessons.contentBody:
Before the knowledge check, confirm you understand the professional-boundary statement below. You will be asked to apply it while answering the quiz questions.

I understand that my role is to report, protect, document and communicate within scope. I do not diagnose bird flu, handle wildlife without authorisation, certify sites as disease-free or make product claims that are not supported by label, SDS, regulator position and evidence.

Certificate disclaimer: Awareness training only. Not veterinary, medical, wildlife handling or government biosecurity response training.

lessons.title: Knowledge check

courses.title: Work Health and Safety (WHS) Fundamentals for Restoration and Cleaning Professionals
courses.description:
EDUCATIONAL & PRE-TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT LEGAL ADVICE. This course is a best-practice educational summary compiled from published research and public regulator guidance, current to July 2026. It is not Australian or New Zealand legal advice or legal representation, does not replace current legislation, Codes of Practice or your regulator's direction, and does not make you licensed or competent for high-risk work. Always verify against the current law and your state or territory WHS regulator before acting.

A CARSI-original, Australian work health and safety (WHS) foundation for restoration, water-damage, mould, fire, biohazard and cleaning technicians. It rebuilds the sound structure of a hazardous-operations safety course — regulatory framework, hazard identification, hazardous materials, PPE, site hazards, decontamination and emergency response — fully translated to Australian law and brought current to July 2026.

You will learn the WHS Act/Regulations and the PCBU duty of care (not US OSHA), the engineered stone ban and the crystalline silica substances regime, asbestos licensing, the 2025 lead blood-level changes, GHS 7 Safety Data Sheets, P2/P3 respiratory selection and fit-testing to AS/NZS 1715/1716, RCD protection on wet sites, confined-space entry, biohazard and mould controls, and notifiable-incident reporting.

This is general WHS awareness training and does not, by itself, confer any licence or high-risk-work competency.
courses.shortDescription: Australian WHS foundations for restoration and cleaning technicians — duty of care, asbestos and silica, PPE, wet-site electrical, confined spaces, biohazard, mould and incident reporting, current to July 2026.

meta.origin: Rebuilt AU-original from a US OSHA/HAZWOPER source document (Google Drive 'CARSI Courses/RTO Courses/OH&S'); the US framework does not apply in Australia and was fully replaced.
meta.australianProduction: AU English, 230V/50Hz, metric, AS/NZS standards, Safe Work Australia + state regulators. Verified against carsi-course-production skill.
meta.cecStatus: NOT an IICRC CEC course. cecHours null. General WHS awareness content; no IICRC CEC claim is made.
meta.officialSourceRecheck: 2026-07-11: engineered stone ban (1 Jul 2024) + import ban (1 Jan 2025), silica CSS regime (1 Sep 2024), WES->WEL (1 Dec 2026, RCS limit under further review), asbestos Class A/B >10m2 threshold (WHS Reg 487), lead removal levels 20/5 ug/dL (WHS Amendment (Blood Lead Level Exposure Values) Regs 2025, F2025L00404), AS/NZS 1715:2009 & 1716:2012 current — all Tier-1 verified via Safe Work Australia / legislation.gov.au / Standards Australia. Recheck before publish: exact RCS WEL figure, any AS/NZS 1715/1716 re-issue, per-state industrial manslaughter penalties.
meta.verificationMethod: nexus deep-research (3 subagents) + Exa Tier-1 corroboration; nexus-copywriter substantiation gate.

modules.title: Before you begin — important notice (read first)

lessons.title: How to use this course — educational, pre-training material only
lessons.contentBody:
PLEASE READ FIRST — THIS IS EDUCATIONAL, PRE-TRAINING MATERIAL ONLY.

This course exists to build foundational awareness of work health and safety for people working in the Australian restoration and cleaning industry. Read it in that spirit:

  • It is not legal advice and not legal representation. It does not create any professional, legal or advisory relationship, and it must not be relied on as a statement of your legal obligations.
  • It has no formal Australian or New Zealand legal endorsement. It is a best-practice educational summary compiled from publicly available research and regulator guidance that was current when written (July 2026). Laws, Codes of Practice, standards and exposure limits change — several referenced here change during and after 2026.
  • The official sources always win. Where anything here differs from the current WHS Act or Regulations, an official Code of Practice, an Australian Standard, a Safety Data Sheet, or a direction from your regulator, follow the official source.
  • It does not make you licensed or competent for high-risk or licensed work (such as asbestos removal or confined-space entry). Those require the relevant units of competency and, where applicable, a regulator-issued licence.
  • Verify before you act. For any real job, confirm the current requirements with Safe Work Australia and your state or territory WHS regulator, and seek professional or legal advice for your specific circumstances.

You will see a short version of this notice at the start of every module — that repetition is deliberate. Treat every section as “learn the concept, then verify the current rule.”

modules.title: The WHS law that applies to you in Australia

lessons.title: How work health and safety is governed in Australia
lessons.contentBody:
EDUCATIONAL & PRE-TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT LEGAL ADVICE. This lesson is a best-practice educational summary compiled from published research and public regulator guidance. It is not Australian or New Zealand legal advice or legal representation, and it does not replace the current legislation, the official Codes of Practice, your regulator’s direction, a Safety Data Sheet, or professional advice for your specific situation. Always verify against the current law and your state or territory WHS regulator before acting.

If you have done a US-style safety course, most of the framework you learned does not apply here. Australia has no OSHA and no single national safety code. Instead we have work health and safety (WHS) law.

Safe Work Australia writes the model WHS Act, model WHS Regulations and model Codes of Practice — but it is a policy body only. It does not inspect, issue notices or prosecute. Enforcement is done by your state or territory regulator: SafeWork NSW, WorkSafe Victoria, Workplace Health and Safety Queensland, WorkSafe WA, SafeWork SA, WorkSafe Tasmania, WorkSafe ACT, NT WorkSafe, or Comcare for national employers.

The model laws have been adopted by every jurisdiction except Victoria (Western Australia was the last to harmonise, on 31 March 2022). Victoria runs its own Occupational Health and Safety Act 2004, enforced by WorkSafe Victoria, and still uses “employer / employee” language rather than the WHS terms below.

Golden rule for a mobile restoration crew: the duties are similar everywhere, but the Act, the section numbers and the reporting line change at the border. Always check the regulator in the state you are working in.

lessons.title: Who owes what duty — PCBU, officers and workers
lessons.contentBody:
Under the model WHS Act the primary duty holder is the PCBU — a Person Conducting a Business or Undertaking. This replaces the old “employer” and is broader; “worker” likewise covers employees, contractors, labour hire and volunteers.

  • PCBU — primary duty of care (s.19): ensure health and safety so far as is reasonably practicable (SFAIRP).
  • Officers — due diligence (s.27): directors and senior managers must take proactive, reasonable steps to ensure the PCBU complies. This is a personal, prosecutable duty.
  • Workers: take reasonable care for your own and others’ safety, follow reasonable instructions and cooperate with policies.

Industrial manslaughter is now an offence in every Australian jurisdiction (Queensland led in 2017; NSW commenced 16 September 2024 and Tasmania on 2 October 2024 completed national coverage). Gross negligence by a PCBU or officer causing a worker’s death carries penalties into the tens of millions of dollars and years of imprisonment.

Psychosocial risk (bullying, violence, harassment, unreasonable job demands) is also a legal WHS duty nationally, with Victoria’s standalone regulations commencing 1 December 2025 — it is not just “wellbeing”.

Terms you will use daily: SWMS (Safe Work Method Statement), JSEA (Job Safety and Environmental Analysis), site induction, White Card (general construction induction, unit CPCWHS1001), and RCD / safety switch.

modules.title: Finding and controlling hazards — the risk management process

lessons.title: Identify, assess, control, review — and the hierarchy of controls
lessons.contentBody:
EDUCATIONAL & PRE-TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT LEGAL ADVICE. This lesson is a best-practice educational summary compiled from published research and public regulator guidance. It is not Australian or New Zealand legal advice or legal representation, and it does not replace the current legislation, the official Codes of Practice, your regulator’s direction, a Safety Data Sheet, or professional advice for your specific situation. Always verify against the current law and your state or territory WHS regulator before acting.

Every WHS duty comes down to one repeatable loop you run on every job:

  1. Identify the hazards (walk the site; think chemical, biological, electrical, physical, psychosocial).
  2. Assess the risk (how likely, how severe).
  3. Control the risk using the hierarchy below.
  4. Review the controls — did they work, did the site change?

The hierarchy of controls is worked top down. PPE is the last line, never the first:

  1. Elimination — remove the hazard (e.g. don’t dry-cut; don’t enter until isolated).
  2. Substitution — a safer product or method.
  3. Isolation — separate people from the hazard (containment, barricades).
  4. Engineering — water suppression, on-tool dust extraction, negative air.
  5. Administrative — procedures, training, signage, rotation.
  6. PPE — respirators, gloves, coveralls (protects one person, only if worn and fitted correctly).
“Reasonably practicable” (SFAIRP) means you weigh the risk against what it takes to control it — but the higher the harm, the more you must do.

lessons.title: SWMS, JSEA and site induction on a restoration job
lessons.contentBody:
A Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS) is legally mandatory before you start high-risk construction work. There are 18 defined categories; the ones restoration crews meet most are work where a person could fall more than 2 m, asbestos disturbance, confined spaces, demolition, and work on or near energised electrical installations. The SWMS names the hazards, the controls and how the work is done, in consultation with the workers doing it.

A JSEA (Job Safety and Environmental Analysis) is a task-by-task risk assessment. It is good practice but not statutory like a SWMS — use it for the many restoration tasks that fall outside high-risk construction work.

Before working on a construction-type site you also need a site-specific induction and, for construction work, a White Card (nationally recognised, valid in every state and territory).

modules.title: Hazardous materials on restoration and strip-out sites

lessons.title: Asbestos — assume it in any pre-2004 building
lessons.contentBody:
EDUCATIONAL & PRE-TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT LEGAL ADVICE. This lesson is a best-practice educational summary compiled from published research and public regulator guidance. It is not Australian or New Zealand legal advice or legal representation, and it does not replace the current legislation, the official Codes of Practice, your regulator’s direction, a Safety Data Sheet, or professional advice for your specific situation. Always verify against the current law and your state or territory WHS regulator before acting.

Australia has had a total ban on manufacturing, supplying and using asbestos since 31 December 2003, so all asbestos work today is legacy removal and management. Any workplace or building constructed before 31 December 2003 must have an asbestos register, and where asbestos is (or is assumed) present, an asbestos management plan.

The rule that protects you: if a material cannot be tested, assume it is asbestos. On a restoration job, be especially careful with cement sheet, vinyl and lino, textured “popcorn” ceilings, pipe lagging, eaves and roofing in older buildings — and remember fire and water damage can turn bonded (non-friable) asbestos into friable asbestos. Check the register, or assume and test, before you cut, sand or demolish.

Removal licensing (model WHS Regulations):

  • Class A licence — removes all asbestos, including friable asbestos and asbestos-contaminated dust.
  • Class B licence — non-friable (bonded) asbestos only, required once you remove more than 10 m².
  • No licence is needed only for 10 m² or less of non-friable asbestos — except in the ACT, where any amount needs a licence. Friable asbestos, any amount, always needs Class A.

Workers need training (asbestos awareness, or units CPCCDE3014 non-friable / CPCCDE3015 friable). The national awareness body is ASEA — the Asbestos and Silica Safety and Eradication Agency (renamed in December 2023 to add silica); note it is not a regulator — enforcement stays with your state WHS regulator.

lessons.title: Silica and the engineered stone ban
lessons.contentBody:
Silica is the fastest-changing hazard in this trade. Two things you must know:

1. Engineered stone is banned. Since 1 July 2024 it is an offence for a PCBU to carry out (or allow) the manufacture, supply, processing or installation of engineered stone benchtops, panels and slabs, in every state and territory. Since 1 January 2025 it is also a prohibited import. Working with legacy engineered stone already installed — repair, minor modification, removal or disposal during a strip-out — is still permitted, but you must notify your WHS regulator before the work and use full silica controls.

2. All crystalline silica is controlled. From 1 September 2024, processing any crystalline silica substance (CSS) — any material with at least 1% crystalline silica, which includes concrete, brick, tile, mortar, grout, natural stone and fibre-cement — without controls is prohibited. If cutting, grinding, drilling or demolishing is assessed as high risk, the PCBU must prepare a written Silica Risk Control Plan before work starts, arrange air monitoring (report to the regulator if the standard is exceeded) and health monitoring, and provide silica-specific training.

The current workplace exposure standard for respirable crystalline silica is 0.05 mg/m³ (8-hour TWA). From 1 December 2026 the “workplace exposure standards (WES)” become “workplace exposure limits (WEL)”; the silica limit itself is under further review, so keep working to 0.05 mg/m³ and watch for the updated figure.

Controls in order: engineering first — on-tool water suppression or H-class dust extraction — then RPE (minimum P2, correctly fit-tested) as the last line, never the only line.

lessons.title: Lead, and chemicals under the GHS
lessons.contentBody:
Lead. Australian house paint could contain up to 50% lead until 1970 (cut to 0.1% only in December 1997), so pre-1970 homes very likely contain high-lead paint. Sanding, scraping, heat-gunning or demolishing painted surfaces makes lead dust and fume. “Lead-risk work” triggers notification to the regulator and health (blood-lead) monitoring. The removal-from-work blood-lead levels were lowered in 2025 to 20 µg/dL for males and females not of reproductive capacity (was 40) and 5 µg/dL for females of reproductive capacity (was 10) — any course still quoting 40/10 is out of date. Controls: assess, contain, wet methods, HEPA, P2 RPE, decontamination; never dry-sand or uncontrolled-blast.

Chemicals. Australia classifies and labels workplace chemicals under the Globally Harmonised System (GHS) Revision 7, mandatory since 1 January 2023. Practical duties on site:

  • Use Safety Data Sheets (SDS) — not the old US term “MSDS” — kept current (reviewed at least every 5 years) and accessible to workers.
  • Keep a register of hazardous chemicals used, handled or stored.
  • Label every container to GHS 7, including anything you decant or site-mix.
  • Placarding and a manifest to emergency services apply above the threshold quantities.

For any exposure or poisoning, Poisons Information is 13 11 26; emergencies are 000.

lessons.title: Choosing the right respirator — P1, P2, P3
lessons.contentBody:
EDUCATIONAL & PRE-TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT LEGAL ADVICE. This lesson is a best-practice educational summary compiled from published research and public regulator guidance. It is not Australian or New Zealand legal advice or legal representation, and it does not replace the current legislation, the official Codes of Practice, your regulator’s direction, a Safety Data Sheet, or professional advice for your specific situation. Always verify against the current law and your state or territory WHS regulator before acting.

Respiratory protection in Australia is governed by two standards: AS/NZS 1715 (selection, use and maintenance — how to run a respirator program) and AS/NZS 1716 (the device standard that defines the filter classes). Any respirator you use for these jobs must be marked to AS/NZS 1716.

  • P1 — coarse mechanically generated dust only; rarely enough for restoration hazards.
  • P2 — fine particulate, fumes and mists. This is the Australian baseline for mould and fire/soot clean-up. (P2 is broadly equivalent to the US “N95”, but in Australia you must specify P2.)
  • P3 — highest particulate protection, but it only delivers its rated protection on a full-face or tight-fitting non-disposable respirator. Required for heavy mould loads inside containment, silica, and significant biohazard or Category 3 aerosols.

Fit-testing vs fit-checking. Any tight-fitting respirator (P2/P3) must be fit-tested to the individual before first use and periodically after, and you must be clean-shaven at the seal. A fit-check (user seal check) is done every single time you put it on — it does not replace fit-testing.

Quick mapping: mould inspection/light work — P2 (P3 full-face in active containment); soot and fire residue — P2 stepping to P3 full-face where combustion products or older materials are disturbed; silica — minimum P2, P3 for higher exposure; biohazard/Cat 3 — P2 floor, P3 full-face where spray or aerosol is likely, with eye and face protection.

lessons.title: The rest of the kit — and a US→AU translation you can trust
lessons.contentBody:
Beyond the respirator, match the rest of the kit to the hazard: fluid-resistant coveralls, gloves (nitrile for chemical/biohazard), eye and face protection, and boots. PPE is issued, maintained and replaced by the PCBU at no cost to the worker.

If your reference material is American, translate it before you rely on it:

US term (wrong here)Correct Australian term / standard
N95 / P100 respiratorP2 / P3 (AS/NZS 1716)
OSHA 1910.134 (respirators)AS/NZS 1715
GFCIRCD / safety switch (AS/NZS 3012, 3760)
120 V / 60 Hz230 V / 50 Hz
MSDSSDS (GHS 7)
HAZWOPER (29 CFR 1910.120)No Australian equivalent — WHS Regs + Codes of Practice
OSHA / PELSafe Work Australia / WES (WEL from Dec 2026)
OSHA 6 ft fall triggerManaging the risk of falls COP (manage any fall risk)
OSHA 90 dBA noise PEL85 dB(A) LAeq,8h + 140 dB(C) peak

lessons.title: Electricity on a wet site
lessons.contentBody:
EDUCATIONAL & PRE-TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT LEGAL ADVICE. This lesson is a best-practice educational summary compiled from published research and public regulator guidance. It is not Australian or New Zealand legal advice or legal representation, and it does not replace the current legislation, the official Codes of Practice, your regulator’s direction, a Safety Data Sheet, or professional advice for your specific situation. Always verify against the current law and your state or territory WHS regulator before acting.

A flooded or stripped building is effectively a construction/demolition environment, so AS/NZS 3012 (electrical installations on construction and demolition sites) and AS/NZS 3760 (in-service testing — “test and tag”) apply to your temporary supply, leads and portable gear.

Australian mains is 230 V at 50 Hz. Water lowers your body’s resistance and creates earth paths, and a current as low as about 30 mA can cause a fatal heart rhythm — which is exactly why every portable tool and lead on a wet site must be protected by a residual current device (RCD / safety switch) rated at 30 mA or less, push-button tested before use.

The property’s own RCDs and switchboard may be damaged by water ingress — do not assume site protection exists. Bring your own portable RCD, and confirm isolation (or have a licensed electrician confirm safe supply) before you enter standing water near energised circuits.

lessons.title: Confined spaces, biohazard and mould
lessons.contentBody:
Confined spaces. Subfloors, crawl spaces, roof voids, tanks, pits and sumps become confined spaces after water damage — restricted entry, not built for occupancy, and prone to oxygen deficiency and hydrogen sulphide or methane from decomposing sewage. Work to AS 2865 and the Safe Work Australia Confined Spaces Code of Practice: risk assessment, entry permit, atmospheric testing (oxygen, flammables, toxics) before and during entry, ventilation, a standby person and a rescue plan. Never run petrol pumps or generators near a confined space — carbon monoxide is a classic restoration fatality.

Biohazard and Category 3 water. There is no single Australian “biohazard standard”; the duty is the general WHS risk-control duty plus state health infection-control practice (there is no local equivalent of the US Bloodborne Pathogens standard). Grossly contaminated (Category 3) water and sewage need full PPE, source control, no aerosolisation, decontamination and tracked waste. The IICRC S500 (water, with its Category 1/2/3 classes) and S540 (trauma) are the recognised industry methods you use to meet your Australian WHS duty — they are not Australian law.

Mould. Likewise there is no single Australian mould standard: the duty is met through WHS risk management, enHealth guidance and state health advice, with IICRC S520 as the recognised industry method. Control moisture first, then contain, then run HEPA-filtered negative air. Where mould is present, dry with air-filtration devices and dehumidifiers — do not run air movers, which aerosolise spores, until the area is contained and cleared to Condition 1.

lessons.title: Heat, manual handling, falls and noise
lessons.contentBody:
Four everyday hazards, each with a Safe Work Australia Code of Practice or guide your state regulator adopts:

  • Working in heat — PPE, coveralls, running drying equipment and un-air-conditioned damaged buildings add up fast. Manage hydration, work/rest cycles, acclimatisation and watch for heat illness.
  • Hazardous manual tasks — dragging saturated carpet and underlay, awkward subfloor postures and repetitive extraction. Control force, repetition, posture and duration.
  • Falls — roof-leak access, ladders, wet surfaces and elevated tanks. Manage any fall risk (historically emphasised above 2 m) using the fall-control hierarchy.
  • Noise — banks of air movers, extraction and cutting tools. The exposure standard is 85 dB(A) averaged over 8 hours, with a 140 dB(C) peak; provide and fit hearing protection (to AS/NZS 1270) when it is exceeded.

lessons.title: Clean and dirty zones, and doffing without cross-contaminating
lessons.contentBody:
EDUCATIONAL & PRE-TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT LEGAL ADVICE. This lesson is a best-practice educational summary compiled from published research and public regulator guidance. It is not Australian or New Zealand legal advice or legal representation, and it does not replace the current legislation, the official Codes of Practice, your regulator’s direction, a Safety Data Sheet, or professional advice for your specific situation. Always verify against the current law and your state or territory WHS regulator before acting.

On any biohazard, sewage or mould job, keep a clear split between the dirty (work) zone, a transition/decontamination zone, and the clean zone. Move people and equipment one way, and doff PPE in the correct order in the transition zone so contamination does not walk into clean areas or your vehicle.

Clean before you disinfect. Disinfectants do not work on soiled surfaces, and you must respect product-claim limits: any therapeutic or disinfection claim (e.g. “kills 99.9%”, “hospital grade”) is regulated by the TGA, and under Australian Consumer Law you must not repeat a claim you cannot substantiate. Use products as labelled.

Waste. Clinical and related (biohazard) waste is regulated at state EPA level — segregate, contain, label and dispose of it through a licensed pathway, and keep your records.

modules.title: Emergencies, first aid and incident reporting

lessons.title: First aid and site emergency planning
lessons.contentBody:
EDUCATIONAL & PRE-TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT LEGAL ADVICE. This lesson is a best-practice educational summary compiled from published research and public regulator guidance. It is not Australian or New Zealand legal advice or legal representation, and it does not replace the current legislation, the official Codes of Practice, your regulator’s direction, a Safety Data Sheet, or professional advice for your specific situation. Always verify against the current law and your state or territory WHS regulator before acting.

Follow the Safe Work Australia First aid in the workplace Code of Practice: enough trained first aiders and suitably stocked kits for the risk, plus task-specific additions restoration needs — eyewash for chemical and biohazard work, and sharps/biohazard spill provisions.

Every site needs an emergency plan under the WHS Regulations: response and evacuation, contact points, first-aid access, and provisions for that job’s specific hazards. For any confined-space entry a rescue plan is mandatory — no entry without it.

Know the numbers: 000 for emergencies, 13 11 26 for the Poisons Information Centre.

lessons.title: Notifiable incidents — report, and preserve the site
lessons.contentBody:
Some incidents must be reported to your state or territory WHS regulator immediately (not to any federal body, and there is no “OSHA 300 log” here). The three notifiable categories are:

  1. the death of a person;
  2. a serious injury or illness — needing immediate in-patient hospital treatment, or immediate treatment for an amputation, serious head/eye/burn injury, degloving, spinal injury, loss of a bodily function or serious laceration, or medical treatment within 48 hours of exposure to a substance;
  3. a dangerous incident — a near-miss that exposed anyone to serious risk (uncontrolled electrical or energy release, collapse, fire or explosion, escape of a hazardous substance).

Two further duties: keep written records for at least 5 years, and preserve the incident site — do not disturb it until an inspector arrives or the regulator directs otherwise, except to help an injured person, remove a deceased person, make the site safe, or as directed by police or the regulator.

In Victoria you report to WorkSafe Victoria under the OHS Act — same idea, different Act and section numbers.

lessons.title: Your WHS professional statement
lessons.contentBody:
EDUCATIONAL & PRE-TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT LEGAL ADVICE. This lesson is a best-practice educational summary compiled from published research and public regulator guidance. It is not Australian or New Zealand legal advice or legal representation, and it does not replace the current legislation, the official Codes of Practice, your regulator’s direction, a Safety Data Sheet, or professional advice for your specific situation. Always verify against the current law and your state or territory WHS regulator before acting.

By completing this course you can state, honestly, that you work to the Australian WHS framework:

I identify hazards and control them by the hierarchy of controls, with PPE as the last line. I know my duties as a worker and my PCBU’s primary duty of care. I treat any pre-2004 building as possibly containing asbestos, and I control crystalline silica. I select P2/P3 respiratory protection to AS/NZS 1716, fit-tested. I protect against 230 V electrical risk with an RCD on a wet site. I do not enter a confined space without a permit and a rescue plan. I report notifiable incidents to my state regulator and preserve the site. I make no product or health claim I cannot substantiate.

This is general WHS awareness training for the Australian restoration and cleaning industry. It is not a licence or a competency for licensed work (such as asbestos removal or confined-space entry), which require the relevant units of competency and, where applicable, a regulator-issued licence.

lessons.title: Knowledge check
